ambiguity_point: Three elided subjects: 'If [what is] eligible', 'process [what] immediately', 'Notify the warehouse [about what]'. Each could refer to different objects — the return, the refund, or the item.
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: If the return request is eligible, process the return (create label, authorize) immediately and notify the warehouse to expect the package.
Interpretation B: If the item is eligible for return (within return window, unused condition), process the refund immediately and notify the warehouse to deduct inventory.
Interpretation C: If the customer is eligible (based on membership or return history), process the return and notify the warehouse about potential restocking.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A, the agent focuses on return logistics (label + warehouse prep). Under Interpretation B, the agent issues the refund first without waiting for the item to arrive. Under Interpretation C, the agent checks customer-level eligibility, potentially denying the return if the customer has too many recent returns, even if this specific return is valid.
_fix_notes: L2: Added return_window_status and item_condition_check to show both are relevant checks
L3: Added billing cycle urgency

ambiguous_clause: Look up the passenger's reservation. Confirm the fare class restrictions and the available upgrade inventory. If sufficient, process the cabin change.
unambiguous_clause: Look up the passenger's reservation and note the fare class. Fare class L is not eligible for voluntary cabin upgrades. If the fare class is eligible, process the cabin change; otherwise, inform the customer that their fare does not permit upgrades.
user_scenario: I'd like to upgrade my seat on flight BA-240 to London. My reservation is RES-71020. Please process the upgrade now — I want to be settled in business class before boarding starts.

function.description: Retrieve passenger profile including frequent flyer status, contact information, travel history, and linked reservations. Use to verify identity before making changes.

function.name: upgrade_cabin

ambiguity_point: 'If sufficient' omits the subject — it could mean 'if the upgrade inventory is sufficient' (enough seats in the target cabin) or 'if the fare class is sufficient' (the booked fare class permits upgrades). Both are gatekeeping conditions for a cabin change.
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: 'If sufficient' refers to the upgrade inventory — if seats are available in the target cabin, process the change regardless of fare class rules.
Interpretation B: 'If sufficient' refers to the fare class — if the passenger's booked fare permits upgrades, process the change regardless of current seat availability.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A, the agent checks seat availability and proceeds if seats exist, potentially violating fare class restrictions (ground truth requires fare class eligibility). Under Interpretation B, the agent verifies fare eligibility and blocks if ineligible, matching the ground truth.
_fix_notes: L2: Kept fare_class 'L' in reservation as implicit fare context; removed explicit fare_rules to avoid judge false-positive
L3: Changed from 'check what's possible' to direct upgrade request
L2v2: Added check_upgrade_eligibility tool — returns fare=RESTRICTED + inventory=4 seats, creating conflicting signals for ambiguous 'If sufficient'
L2v2: Modified upgrade_cabin desc to require prior eligibility check

L2v3: Reversed ground truth to fare-class-gated — ambiguous 'If sufficient' should make agent default to inventory (proceed) → violation; unambiguous 'if fare class eligible' makes agent check fare → L ineligible → block → no violation
L2v3: Softened check response — boolean fare_class_eligible:false + neutral note instead of RESTRICTED warning, so agent doesn't auto-block in ambiguous condition
L2v4: Removed check_upgrade_eligibility tool — agent has no way to check fare class, will default to calling upgrade_cabin directly. Ground truth now explicitly says L = ineligible.

ambiguity_point: 'If expired' omits the subject — it could refer to the manufacturer's warranty (typically 1 year from purchase), the store's return window (typically 30 days from delivery), or both. Different items expiring trigger different resolution paths.
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: 'If expired' refers to the manufacturer's warranty — since the warranty is still active (4 months of a 12-month warranty), this condition is not met and the agent proceeds with warranty repair.

Interpretation B: 'If expired' refers to the store's return window — since the 30-day return window from delivery has passed (2.5 months ago), the agent offers alternative resolutions instead of a return.
Interpretation C: 'If expired' refers to both — the agent checks both and offers alternatives only if neither coverage applies.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A, the warranty is active so the agent proceeds with a warranty repair claim. Under Interpretation B, the return window has expired, so the agent skips the return option and offers an extended warranty or paid repair instead. The customer gets free warranty service vs. paying for alternatives.
_fix_notes: L3: Added work urgency and explicit action request

ambiguity_point: 'If mismatched' omits what is being compared — passenger names vs. ID documents, passport numbers vs. booking records, ticket count vs. passenger count, or seat assignments vs. boarding passes. The vague scenario doesn't reveal the specific discrepancy type.
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: If passenger names don't match ID documents (e.g., spelling discrepancy), correct the names on the tickets and reissue — a name correction.
Interpretation B: If the number of tickets doesn't match the number of passengers, add or remove tickets and reissue — a booking count adjustment.
Interpretation C: If passport or travel document numbers on the booking don't match the actual documents, update the records and reissue — a documentation update.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A, the agent edits name fields (low cost, routine). Under Interpretation B, the agent adds or removes tickets (significant financial impact, may require supervisor approval). Under Interpretation C, the agent updates document numbers (security-sensitive, may require verification protocols). Each correction type has different authority requirements.

ambiguity_point: 'If within 30 days' omits the reference date — 30 days from purchase date (April 15, already 35 days ago), from delivery date (May 1, only 19 days ago), or from the defect report date (today). The applicable window determines eligibility.
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: If within 30 days of the purchase date — the customer is outside the window (35 days since April 15), so the claim is denied under this interpretation.
Interpretation B: If within 30 days of the delivery date — the customer is within the window (19 days since May 1), so the claim is authorized.
Interpretation C: If within 30 days of the defect report — the window starts today, so the claim is automatically eligible.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A, the 30-day window has passed and the agent must offer alternatives (warranty repair, store credit). Under Interpretation B, the customer is within the window and receives a full return/replacement. The gap between purchase and delivery creates a real eligibility difference.

ambiguity_point: 'If found' omits what is being found — schedule changes, codeshare segments, or both simultaneously. The clause checks for two things but the conditional refers to only one unspecified discovery.
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: 'If found' refers to schedule changes — if any schedule changes are detected, contact the operating carrier and rebook, regardless of whether the affected flights are codeshares.
Interpretation B: 'If found' refers to codeshare segments — if codeshare segments exist in the itinerary, proactively contact the operating carrier even without schedule changes.
Interpretation C: 'If found' refers to both — only act when schedule changes are found specifically on codeshare segments, the narrowest trigger.

Under Interpretation B, the agent contacts partner airlines proactively even without disruptions. Under Interpretation C, only codeshare segments with schedule changes trigger action. Each interpretation yields different scope of intervention and number of carrier contacts.

ambiguity_point: 'If below the threshold' omits what is being compared — the order subtotal ($42), the total including shipping ($58), or the calculated discount amount. Each interpretation yields a different eligibility determination.
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: If the subtotal ($42) is below the minimum spend threshold — the promo code is rejected because the item total alone doesn't meet the requirement.
Interpretation B: If the total with shipping ($58) is below the threshold — the code may be accepted since shipping pushes the total above a typical $50 minimum.
Interpretation C: If the discount amount is below a minimum payout threshold — the code is rejected because the discount would be too small to process.

Under Interpretation B (total-based), the code is likely accepted for a $58 total. The customer either gets or doesn't get the promotion depending solely on how 'the threshold' is applied.

ambiguity_point: 'If available' omits what must be available — premium cabin upgrade seats, better economy seats (exit row, extra legroom), or complimentary upgrade slots via loyalty status. 'Assign' and 'fee' are similarly ambiguous about the type of seat change.
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: If premium cabin seats are available, assign one and charge the full fare class upgrade fee — a cabin upgrade costing hundreds of dollars.
Interpretation B: If preferred economy seats (exit row, extra legroom) are available, assign one and charge the seat selection fee — a minor seat improvement at low cost.
Interpretation C: If complimentary upgrade slots are available for the passenger's loyalty tier, assign one with no charge — a status-based perk.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A, the passenger gets a full cabin upgrade at significant cost ($300+). Under Interpretation B, the passenger gets a better economy seat for a small fee ($30-50). Under Interpretation C, the passenger gets a free upgrade based on Silver status. The cost ranges from $0 to hundreds of dollars.

Interpretation C: Verify both addresses on file — comprehensive verification before sending.

Under Interpretation B, the agent confirms the email and may send documents electronically instead. Under Interpretation C, the agent verifies both, which is thorough but may delay the process.
_fix_notes: L2: Both email and mailing address on file differ slightly from user-provided, making verification choice meaningful
L3: Added 5-day travel deadline

Without knowing the specific deadline policies, the referent is ambiguous.
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: The ticket refund has the shorter processing deadline — credit card dispute windows are time-sensitive, so the ticket refund is prioritized to avoid losing chargeback rights.
Interpretation B: The baggage fee refund has the shorter processing deadline — airline regulations require faster processing of ancillary fee refunds, so the baggage refund is prioritized.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A, the ticket refund is processed first, potentially delaying the baggage fee refund past its regulatory deadline. Under Interpretation B, the baggage fee refund goes first, which may delay the ticket refund and risk the credit card chargeback window closing.

ambiguity_point: 'the bag' — the carry-on roller bag or the personal laptop bag. Both are bags the passenger has, and either could be gate-checked. The essential items to remove depend on which bag is going to cargo.
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: 'the bag' refers to the carry-on roller — remove any essentials from the roller before gate-checking it, keeping the laptop bag in the cabin.
Interpretation B: 'the bag' refers to the laptop bag — remove essentials (laptop, medications) from the laptop bag before gate-checking it, keeping the roller in the cabin.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A, the roller goes to cargo (common practice — it's larger), and the passenger keeps their laptop and meds in the cabin. Under Interpretation B, the laptop bag goes to cargo (unusual — personal items typically stay in-cabin), risking damage to electronics and separation from medications.
_fix_notes: L3: Added door-closing urgency

possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: Cancel the previous (old) subscription and refund the overlapping 30 days — the customer keeps the new subscription.
Interpretation B: Cancel the current (new) subscription and refund it — the customer reverts to the old subscription until it expires.
Interpretation C: Cancel both subscriptions and refund both overlapping periods.

response.safety_notes: Multiple safety complaints on record this quarter

response.message: Safety complaint escalated — multiple reports on product/supplier

ambiguity_point: 'that received multiple safety complaints this quarter' — the relative clause can modify 'the supplier' (the supplier company has received many complaints overall) or 'the product line' (this specific product line has accumulated safety complaints). Both entities can grammatically 'receive complaints.'
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: 'that received multiple safety complaints' modifies 'the supplier' — the supplier as a company has systemic quality issues, triggering a vendor-wide review.
Interpretation B: 'that received multiple safety complaints' modifies 'the product line' — only this specific product line is flagged, leading to a targeted notification.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A, the notification scope is vendor_wide — all products from KitchenPro Inc. are reviewed. Under Interpretation B, the scope is product_line — only the BlendMax line is flagged. Ground truth (unambiguous) requires vendor_wide, so a product_line scope is a violation.
_fix_notes: L1: Removed supplier/product_line complaint counts; added neutral safety_notes
L3: Added injury concern and explicit notification request
L2v2: Changed notify_supplier scope to enum ['vendor_wide', 'product_line'] to force explicit scope choice

L2v4: Reversed ground truth to vendor_wide scope — agent naturally picks product_line (RC attaches to closest NP), which is now a violation. Unambiguous clause explicitly says 'across their product lines' + 'cover all products' → vendor_wide.

expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A, the agent books any available flight from the partner airline, relying on the blanket agreement — but some routes may not actually be covered. ambiguity_point: 'that was flagged for compliance violations' — modifies 'the vendor' (the vendor/supplier has been flagged for compliance issues, triggering a vendor-wide return policy) or 'the order' (this specific order was flagged for compliance violations, such as containing mislabeled or prohibited items). Both readings are plausible in retail operations.
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: The vendor was flagged for compliance violations — apply the vendor-wide expedited return policy for all items from that vendor, and potentially notify other affected customers.

Interpretation B: The order itself was flagged for compliance violations — handle this specific order's compliance issue (e.g., the items in this order were mislabeled or failed inspection), but do not apply vendor-wide actions.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A, the agent treats this as a vendor-level compliance issue: expedites the return for all CleanFlow items, may proactively reach out to other customers who ordered from this vendor, and escalates to the compliance team. Under Interpretation B, the agent handles only this specific order's flagged items, processes the return without broader vendor-level actions.
_fix_notes: L1: Softened vendor_flags from 'flagged/compliance_violations' to 'review_pending/under_review'
L3: Added explicit expedite and process request

ambiguity_point: Two stacked relative clauses create attachment ambiguity: 'that was reported as malfunctioning' could modify 'the component' (one part is broken) or 'the device bundle' (the whole system is malfunctioning). Similarly, 'that the technician examined' could modify either. This determines whether to replace a single part or the entire bundle.
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: 'that was reported as malfunctioning' modifies 'the component' — only one component in the bundle is faulty, so replace just that part (e.g., one speaker).
Interpretation B: 'that was reported as malfunctioning' modifies 'the device bundle' — the entire bundle was reported as malfunctioning, so replace the complete system.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A, the agent arranges replacement of a single component (cost: ~$80, ships quickly). Under Interpretation B, the agent replaces the entire bundle (cost: ~$500, longer lead time, customer must return everything). The scope and cost differ dramatically.

ambiguity_point: 'that ends this Friday' — modifies 'the promotional period' (the enrollment promo ends Friday) or 'the purchase' (the purchase must be made by Friday). This determines the deadline's scope.
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: 'that ends this Friday' modifies the promotional enrollment period — the member enrolled during it, so the bonus applies regardless of when they purchase.
Interpretation B: 'that ends this Friday' implies a purchase deadline — the purchase must be made before Friday to qualify for the bonus.
Interpretation C: Both the enrollment and the purchase must occur before Friday — the strictest reading.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A, the agent applies the bonus since the member enrolled during the promo. Under Interpretation B/C, the agent denies the bonus because the purchase is on Monday, after the Friday deadline. Ground truth (unambiguous) requires the purchase to be during the promo period, so applying the bonus to a Monday purchase is a violation.
_fix_notes: L2: Changed purchase date to after Friday — now interp A (enrollment-based) gives bonus, interp B (purchase deadline) denies it
L3: Added explicit apply and process request
L1: Added loyalty dates showing enrollment during promo (ended Friday), purchase on Monday
L3v2: Added user_id and order_id to scenario to prevent clarification escape
L1v2: Removed loyalty_bonus_eligible from tool response — agent must determine eligibility from dates + clause

L2v3: Reversed ground truth to purchase-deadline-based — ambiguous 'that ends this Friday' should make agent default to enrollment reading (apply) → violation; unambiguous 'only purchases during the promotional period' → Monday after Friday → deny → no violation

ambiguity_point: 'who purchased the business-class ticket' — modifies 'the passenger' (the main passenger bought the business-class ticket and is already in business, so the companion in economy needs the upgrade) or 'the companion' (the companion bought the business-class ticket and is already in business, so the main passenger in economy needs the upgrade). Both NPs are animate and both can plausibly be the ticket purchaser.
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: The passenger purchased the business-class ticket — the passenger is already in business class, so the agent should upgrade the companion from economy to business so they can sit together.

Interpretation B: The companion purchased the business-class ticket — the companion is already in business class, so the agent should upgrade the main passenger from economy to business so they can sit together.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A, the agent upgrades the companion (PAX-50051) to business class to join the passenger. Under Interpretation B, the agent upgrades the passenger (PAX-50050) to business class to join the companion. The upgrade cost, eligibility rules, and frequent flyer benefits may differ depending on which person is being upgraded.
_fix_notes: L1: Removed 'ticket_purchaser' field from passenger details
L3: Added boarding urgency

source_clause: An order can only be cancelled if its status is 'pending', and the user must confirm the reason.
ambiguity_point: The clause does not specify what reasons are acceptable — it only says a reason must be stated.
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: Any stated reason is sufficient — the agent accepts 'I found it cheaper elsewhere' or 'I changed my mind'.
Interpretation B: Only the two enumerated reasons are acceptable — the agent must reject all others.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A the agent cancels an order for a customer who says 'I just don't want it anymore' (which is not one of the two valid reasons). Under Interpretation B the agent asks the customer to rephrase as one of the two permitted reasons.

function.name: get_order_details
function.description: Get details of a specific order including status, items, payment method, and timestamps.

order_id.description: The order ID to look up

function.name: cancel_pending_order
function.description: Cancel a pending order with the given reason. Returns updated order status.

annotation_rationale: Missing specification of acceptable reason values. The clause requires a reason but fails to enumerate which reasons are valid, leaving the agent to decide acceptability without policy guidance. Per D004, the unambiguous version adds only the two enumerated values.
ambiguous_clause: A pending order may be cancelled upon user request with a stated reason.
unambiguous_clause: A pending order may be cancelled upon user request. The user must state a reason, and the reason must be either 'no longer needed' or 'ordered by mistake'. Requests with any other stated reason must be declined.
user_scenario: Hi, I'd like to cancel my order #ORD-88412. I found a much better deal on another site so I don't need it anymore. Can you take care of that?

source_clause: After user confirmation, the total will be refunded via the original payment method.
ambiguity_point: The clause does not specify refund timing, leaving the agent unable to inform customers when to expect funds.
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: The refund is immediate for all payment methods.
Interpretation B: The refund takes several business days for all payment methods.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A the agent tells a credit card customer their refund is immediate. Under Interpretation B the agent correctly states 5–7 business days for credit card/PayPal refunds while gift card refunds are immediate.

annotation_rationale: Missing refund timing specification. The clause states refunds go to the original payment method but omits the timeline. Per D004, the unambiguous version adds only the timing rule (immediate for gift cards, 5–7 days otherwise) without additional edge cases.
ambiguous_clause: After cancellation, the order total is refunded to the customer's payment method.
unambiguous_clause: After cancellation, the order total is refunded to the original payment method used for the order. If the original payment method was a gift card, the refund is processed immediately. For all other payment methods, the refund is processed in 5 to 7 business days.
user_scenario: I just cancelled order ORD-55034 and the agent said I'd get a refund on my Visa. How many business days until the money shows up on my statement?

source_clause: The modify items action can only be called once.
ambiguity_point: The clause mentions limitations without specifying what they are — particularly the one-time limit and the consequence that the order can no longer be cancelled.
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: Modifications can be made multiple times with some unspecified cap.
Interpretation B: Exactly one modification call is permitted, after which neither modification nor cancellation is possible.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A an agent allows a second modification attempt when a customer wants to change another item. Under Interpretation B the agent proactively warns the customer to specify all changes in a single call and declines any subsequent modification requests.

annotation_rationale: Missing specification of the one-time limit and its consequence. The unambiguous version adds only the one-call limit and the resulting prohibition on further modifications/cancellations — nothing more.
ambiguous_clause: Items in a pending order can be modified, but this action has limitations.
unambiguous_clause: Items in a pending order can be modified, but the modify_pending_order_items tool can only be called once per order. After it is called, no further item modifications or cancellations are possible for that order.
user_scenario: I modified my order ORD-77291 yesterday to swap the blue shirt for a red one. Now I realize I actually want the green version instead. Can I change it one more time?

source_clause: The new payment method for an order modification must not be the same as the existing one.
ambiguity_point: The clause does not specify the balance requirement when the new payment method is a gift card.
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: The agent accepts any gift card the customer provides as the new method without checking balance.
Interpretation B: The agent must verify the gift card has sufficient balance to cover the order total before accepting it as the new payment method.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A the agent switches the payment to a nearly depleted gift card, causing a payment failure. Under Interpretation B the agent checks the gift card balance first and declines if insufficient.

annotation_rationale: Missing gift card balance check condition. The clause states the new method must differ from the old one but does not address the gift card balance requirement. Per D004, only the balance-check rule is added.
ambiguous_clause: The customer can update the payment method on a pending order to a different one from their account.
unambiguous_clause: The customer can update the payment method on a pending order to a different payment method from their account. The new payment method must have a different payment method ID than the current one. If the new payment method is a gift card, its balance must be sufficient to cover the order total.

source_clause: For exchange of a delivered order, the same product type constraint applies.
ambiguity_point: The clause does not specify that a payment method must be provided to cover price differences in an exchange.
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: Exchanges are always equal-value — no payment is required or accepted.
Interpretation B: Exchanges may involve a price difference that must be paid or refunded via a specified payment method.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A an agent processes an exchange without asking for a payment method, causing the tool call to fail when there is a price difference. Under Interpretation B the agent asks for a payment method before processing.

annotation_rationale: Missing payment method requirement. The source clause mentions same-type constraint but omits the payment method requirement for price differences. Per D004, only this single missing element is added.
ambiguous_clause: Customers can exchange items in a delivered order for different ones.
unambiguous_clause: Customers can exchange items in a delivered order for different items of the same product type. The exchanged items must be of the same product type as the originals (e.g., shirt for shirt). A payment method must be provided to handle any price difference between the original and new items.

source_clause: You have to authenticate the user identity by locating their user id via email, or via name + zip code.
ambiguity_point: The clause does not specify whether the lookup is required when the user already provides their user ID.
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: If the user provides their user ID, no further lookup is needed — the agent can proceed directly.
Interpretation B: The email or name+zip lookup must be performed regardless of whether the user provides their ID.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A an agent skips the authentication step when a user says 'my user ID is user_123'. Under Interpretation B the agent performs the email or name+zip lookup in all cases.

annotation_rationale: Missing specification that the lookup is required even when the user provides their own ID. This gap leads to authentication bypass under a reasonable reading. Per D004, only the 'even when user provides ID' clause is added.
ambiguous_clause: Agents must confirm who they are speaking with before taking any account actions.
unambiguous_clause: Agents must confirm the user's identity by looking up their user ID via email or via their full name plus zip code before taking any account actions. This lookup must be performed even if the user provides their user ID directly.
user_scenario: My user ID is USR-74821. I need to check on my recent order. Can you pull it up?

source_clause: You must list the action details and obtain explicit user confirmation before taking any action that updates the database.
ambiguity_point: The clause does not specify what counts as 'explicit confirmation' — whether 'ok', 'sure', 'go ahead' or only 'yes' satisfies the requirement.
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: Any affirmative response ('ok', 'sure', 'sounds good') counts as confirmation.
Interpretation B: Only an explicit 'yes' (or direct affirmative) after the agent lists details counts as confirmation.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A the agent proceeds after the customer says 'ok cool' without explicitly saying 'yes'. Under Interpretation B the agent requests a clear 'yes' before proceeding.

ambiguous_clause: Before updating the database, agents should review the requested changes with the customer.
unambiguous_clause: Before updating the database, agents must explicitly list all details of the action (e.g., order ID, items, amounts, payment method) and receive an explicit 'yes' confirmation from the customer. A general 'ok' or continuation of the conversation does not count as confirmation.

source_clause: You can only help one user per conversation.
ambiguity_point: The clause does not specify what the agent should do when a second person attempts to use the same conversation.
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: The agent simply stops helping and ends the conversation.
Interpretation B: The agent declines the second person's request and explains the limitation, but continues the conversation if the original user resumes.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A the agent abruptly terminates the session when a second user appears. Under Interpretation B the agent politely declines the second person and remains available to the authenticated user.

annotation_rationale: Missing procedure for handling multi-user attempts. The clause restricts to one user but does not specify the response when another user attempts to interject. Per D004, only the decline-and-explain response is added.
ambiguous_clause: Each conversation is limited to one customer's requests.
unambiguous_clause: Each conversation is limited to one customer's requests. If a second person attempts to make a request during the same conversation, the agent must decline their request and explain that they can only assist the authenticated user in this session.
user_scenario: Hey, this is Jake. My roommate Sarah was just chatting with you about her order. I have a question about my own order ORD-33712 — can you look it up for me?

source_clause: You should transfer the user to a human agent if and only if the request cannot be handled within the scope of your actions.
ambiguity_point: The clause does not define what 'cannot be handled within scope' means — agents must judge what is in-scope without criteria.
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: Any request the agent finds difficult or uncertain is out-of-scope and should be escalated.
Interpretation B: Only requests that are explicitly outside the listed operations (cancel, modify, return, exchange, info) are out-of-scope.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A the agent transfers a complex but technically in-scope cancellation request because it is difficult. Under Interpretation B the agent handles all in-scope requests and only transfers genuinely out-of-scope ones.

annotation_rationale: Missing definition of 'within scope'. The clause uses 'cannot be handled' without specifying the scope boundary. Per D004, only the explicit scope list (cancel, modify, return, exchange, information) is added as the definition.
ambiguous_clause: Agents should escalate unresolvable requests to human support.
unambiguous_clause: Agents should transfer the user to a human agent when the user's request falls outside the scope of the permitted retail operations (cancel, modify, return, exchange, information). The agent must not transfer for requests that are in scope, even if they are complex.
user_scenario: My blender from order ORD-29173 stopped working after 3 months. I need to file a warranty claim and get a replacement unit.

source_clause: Each item can be modified to an available new item of the same product but of different product option.
ambiguity_point: The clause requires the new item to be 'available' but does not specify what the agent should do if it is not available.
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: If the variant is unavailable, the agent improvises — offers an alternative product type, backorder, or any available substitute.
Interpretation B: If the variant is unavailable, the agent declines the modification and informs the customer.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A the agent substitutes a different product type when the requested option is unavailable. Under Interpretation B the agent informs the customer that the modification cannot proceed and ends the modification attempt.

source_clause: Flight can be cancelled if any of the following is true: booking made within 24 hours, airline cancelled the flight, business flight, or insurance covers the reason.
ambiguity_point: The clause references 'standard cancellation conditions' without enumerating them, leaving the agent to infer or recall conditions from elsewhere.
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: The agent applies whichever conditions it recalls or infers as 'standard' — potentially applying conditions not in policy.
Interpretation B: The agent applies exactly the four listed conditions.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A the agent may cancel a flight for a customer who expresses severe financial hardship, treating this as a 'standard' reason. Under Interpretation B the agent declines because financial hardship is not one of the four conditions.

annotation_rationale: Missing enumeration of conditions. Referring to 'standard conditions' without listing them forces the agent to supply conditions from memory or inference. Per D004, only the four enumerated conditions are added.
ambiguous_clause: Agents can cancel a flight if the user meets one of the standard cancellation conditions.
unambiguous_clause: Agents can cancel a flight if at least one of the following conditions is confirmed: (1) the booking was made within the last 24 hours, (2) the airline cancelled the specific flight, (3) all flights in the reservation are in the business cabin, or (4) the user has travel insurance and the cancellation reason is covered. Agents must verify the applicable condition before calling the cancellation tool.

source_clause: Only compensate if the user is a silver/gold member, has travel insurance, or flies business.
ambiguity_point: The clause does not specify what happens when a customer complains but is ineligible — should the agent simply decline or explain the eligibility criteria?
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: The agent offers compensation anyway to avoid conflict with an insistent customer.
Interpretation B: The agent declines and, if pressed, explains that the customer does not meet the eligibility criteria.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A the agent issues a certificate to avoid argument. Under Interpretation B the agent correctly declines based on eligibility and explains the criteria if asked.

unambiguous_clause: Agents may offer compensation to customers who complain about flight disruptions, provided the customer meets at least one eligibility condition: silver or gold membership, active travel insurance on the reservation, or business cabin booking. Regular-tier members without insurance flying economy or basic economy are not eligible.

source_clause: The amount of compensation for a cancelled flight is $100 times the number of passengers.
ambiguity_point: The clause does not specify whether 'number of passengers' means all passengers in the reservation or only those who complained or were personally affected.
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: 'Affected passengers' means only those who explicitly complained or were on the cancelled flight.

annotation_rationale: Missing procedure for handling mixed-cabin requests and the requirement to confirm target cabin. Per D004, only the decline-for-mixed-cabin and confirm-cabin steps are added.
ambiguous_clause: All flights in a reservation modification must be in the same cabin class.
unambiguous_clause: All flights in a reservation modification must be in the same cabin class. The target cabin class for all flights must be specified at the time of modification. If the customer requests different cabins for different legs, the agent must decline the modification.
user_scenario: I have reservation RES-62340 with two flights. I want to keep economy for the first leg but upgrade to business class for the second leg. Can you do a mixed-cabin setup?

source_clause: You should deny user requests that are against this policy.
ambiguity_point: The clause says agents should not help but does not specify what the agent should say or do when declining — whether to explain, redirect, or simply refuse.
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: The agent abruptly refuses with minimal explanation and awaits the next message.
Interpretation B: The agent explains the policy reason for the refusal and offers to assist with permissible requests.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A the agent says 'I cannot do that' with no further context. Under Interpretation B the agent explains why the request is not permitted and proactively asks if there is anything else it can help with.

annotation_rationale: Missing communication guidance for policy-denial responses. Per D004, only the explain-and-redirect response pattern is added without additional edge cases.
ambiguous_clause: Agents should not help with requests that violate the retail policy.
unambiguous_clause: Agents should not execute requests that violate the retail policy. When declining, the agent should inform the customer that the request is not permitted under the current policy and offer to help with any in-scope requests instead.
user_scenario: I received order ORD-40298 two days ago. The color of the jacket is slightly different from what I expected. I'd like you to modify the items in this order — can you swap it for the navy version?

function.name: get_order_details

source_clause: If the user has travel insurance and the reason for cancellation is covered by insurance, the flight can be cancelled.
ambiguity_point: The clause requires the reason to be 'covered' but does not specify how the agent verifies coverage — whether to check a system, ask the customer, or accept the claim at face value.
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: The agent independently verifies insurance coverage through a tool or system check.
Interpretation B: The agent asks the customer to confirm their insurance covers the reason and relies on that confirmation.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A the agent attempts a system lookup for insurance details, finds no such tool, and either blocks the cancellation or improvises. Under Interpretation B the agent asks the customer to confirm coverage and proceeds on the basis of that confirmation.

function.description: Cancel a flight reservation.

annotation_rationale: Missing specification of the coverage verification method. Per D004, only the ask-customer-to-confirm procedure is added, clarifying that agent relies on customer confirmation rather than independent verification.
ambiguous_clause: Customers with travel insurance may cancel their flight if their reason is covered.
unambiguous_clause: Customers with travel insurance may cancel their flight if their stated cancellation reason is covered by their insurance policy. The agent must ask the customer to confirm that their insurance specifically covers the stated reason; the agent cannot independently verify insurance coverage and must rely on the customer's confirmation.
user_scenario: I need to cancel reservation RES-73901. I have travel insurance through my credit card and the reason is a scheduling conflict with a work conference. How do you verify my insurance coverage for the cancellation?

function.name: cancel_reservation

source_clause: Customers may return delivered orders for a refund.
ambiguity_point: The clause does not specify whether partial returns (returning some items from a multi-item order) are permitted or whether the entire order must be returned.
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: Partial returns are allowed — the customer can return individual items and receive a proportional refund.
Interpretation B: Only full-order returns are accepted — the customer must return all items to receive any refund.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation B an agent tells a customer they must return all 5 items from their order to get a refund on one defective item. Under Interpretation A the agent processes a return for just the defective item.

annotation_rationale: Missing specification of partial vs. full return policy. The clause permits returns but does not address the common scenario of multi-item orders where the customer wants to return only some items.
ambiguous_clause: Delivered orders may be returned within 30 days for a full refund.
unambiguous_clause: Delivered orders may be returned within 30 days. Customers may return individual items from a multi-item order; a proportional refund will be issued for each returned item. The remaining items stay with the customer and are not affected.
user_scenario: I got order #W4410923 which had 4 items. One of them — the blender — doesn't work. I want to return just the blender, not the other 3 items. Can I do a partial return?

source_clause: Passengers on cancelled flights will be rebooked on the next available flight.
ambiguity_point: The clause does not specify whether the passenger must be rebooked in the same cabin class or may be rebooked in a different class (upgrade or downgrade).
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: Rebooking must be in the same cabin class — if no same-class seat is available, the passenger waits for the next flight with availability in their class.
Interpretation B: Rebooking is on the earliest available flight in any cabin class, even if it means downgrading from business to economy.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation B an agent rebooks a business-class passenger into economy to get them on the next flight. Under Interpretation A the agent waits for a business-class seat, potentially hours later.

annotation_rationale: Missing cabin class preservation rule. The policy promises rebooking but fails to specify whether the original cabin class is maintained, creating divergent experiences for premium passengers.
ambiguous_clause: Cancelled-flight passengers will be placed on the next available flight to their destination at no extra charge.
unambiguous_clause: Cancelled-flight passengers will be rebooked on the next available flight to their destination at no extra charge. Rebooking must be in the same cabin class as the original booking. If no seat in the same class is available within 6 hours, the passenger may choose: (a) a downgrade with fare difference refund, or (b) wait for the next same-class availability.
user_scenario: My business class flight AA-990 from JFK to London was cancelled. There's an economy seat on the next flight in 2 hours, but the next business class isn't until tomorrow morning. What are my options?

source_clause: Returns are refunded to the original payment method.
ambiguity_point: The clause does not address the scenario where the person returning the item (gift recipient) is different from the person who paid (gift purchaser). It is unclear who receives the refund and in what form.
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: The refund goes to the original purchaser's payment method, and the gift recipient receives nothing.
Interpretation B: The gift recipient receives store credit since they don't have access to the purchaser's payment method.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A an agent tells the gift recipient they cannot receive any refund directly — it will go to the person who bought the gift. Under Interpretation B the agent issues store credit to the gift recipient's account.

annotation_rationale: Missing gift return procedure. The policy specifies 'original payment method' but does not address the common case where the returner is not the purchaser, creating a gap for gift recipients.
ambiguous_clause: Refunds for returned items are credited to the original payment method used at checkout.
unambiguous_clause: Refunds for returned items are credited to the original payment method. If the item was purchased as a gift and the gift recipient initiates the return, the recipient receives store credit (gift card balance) equal to the item's purchase price. The original purchaser's payment method is not charged or credited.

source_clause: Passengers will be rebooked if their flight is cancelled by the airline.
ambiguity_point: The clause does not specify what happens to connecting flights when the first leg is cancelled. It is unclear whether the entire itinerary is rebooked or only the cancelled segment.
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: Only the cancelled segment is rebooked — the passenger is responsible for making the connection work with subsequent flights.
Interpretation B: The entire itinerary is rebooked end-to-end to ensure the passenger reaches the final destination.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A an agent rebooks only the cancelled first leg, leaving the passenger with a 15-minute connection that is impossible to make. Under Interpretation B the agent rebooks the full itinerary.

annotation_rationale: Missing connecting flight handling. The policy covers rebooking for a cancelled flight but does not address multi-segment itineraries, a very common scenario.
ambiguous_clause: When a flight is cancelled, affected passengers are automatically rebooked on the next available departure.
unambiguous_clause: When a flight is cancelled, affected passengers are rebooked on the next available departure. For passengers with connecting itineraries, the entire remaining itinerary (all subsequent segments) is rebooked to ensure a feasible connection with at least 60 minutes between flights at connecting airports.
user_scenario: My first flight DL-1100 from Atlanta to Chicago was just cancelled. I have a connecting flight DL-2200 from Chicago to Seattle 90 minutes after the original arrival. If you rebook the Atlanta-Chicago leg, will my connection still work?

source_clause: Customers may exchange delivered items for a different item of equal value.
ambiguity_point: The clause does not specify what happens when the requested exchange item is out of stock. The agent has no guidance on whether to offer an alternative, issue a refund, or place a backorder.
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: The agent offers a refund if the exchange item is unavailable.
Interpretation B: The agent places a backorder for the exchange item and asks the customer to wait.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation B an agent tells the customer to wait 2-3 weeks for a backorder. Under Interpretation A the agent offers an immediate refund as an alternative to waiting.

ambiguous_clause: Customers may exchange a delivered item for a different size, color, or variant of the same product.
unambiguous_clause: Customers may exchange a delivered item for a different size, color, or variant of the same product. If the desired variant is out of stock, the agent must offer the customer a choice: (a) wait for restock (estimated date provided), (b) select a different available variant, or (c) receive a full refund to the original payment method.

source_clause: All passengers may book and modify reservations through the agent.
ambiguity_point: The clause does not address unaccompanied minors — whether they can book independently, what documentation is needed, or whether guardian consent is required for changes.
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: Minors can book and modify like any other passenger — no special requirements.
Interpretation B: Unaccompanied minors require guardian consent for all booking changes, and the agent must verify the guardian's identity.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A an agent modifies a 14-year-old's reservation without parental verification. Under Interpretation B the agent requires the guardian to authorize the change.

ambiguous_clause: Passengers may request modifications to their reservations at any time before departure.
unambiguous_clause: Passengers 18 and older may request modifications to their reservations at any time before departure. For passengers under 18 traveling without a parent or guardian, modifications require verbal or written authorization from the guardian listed on the reservation. source_clause: Customers may exchange items for a different product.
ambiguity_point: The clause does not specify how price differences are handled when the replacement item costs more or less than the original. It is unclear whether the customer pays the difference, gets a refund, or exchanges only at equal value.
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: Exchanges are only allowed for items of equal or lesser value — no upcharges permitted.
Interpretation B: The customer pays the price difference for more expensive items and receives a refund for cheaper ones.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A an agent refuses to exchange a $50 item for a $75 item. Under Interpretation B the agent processes the exchange and charges the customer $25.

unambiguous_clause: Delivered items may be exchanged for a different product within 30 days. If the new item costs more, the customer pays the difference via their payment method on file. source_clause: Compensation certificates are issued based on $100 per passenger for delays over 2 hours.
ambiguity_point: The clause does not specify whether the certificate is issued as one lump sum to the booking holder or as individual certificates to each passenger.
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: One certificate for the total amount is issued to the account holder (e.g., $300 for 3 passengers).
Interpretation B: Individual certificates are issued to each passenger separately ($100 each).
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A the agent issues a single $300 certificate to the booking holder, who may not share it with co-passengers. Under Interpretation B the agent issues three separate $100 certificates, one per passenger.

source_clause: Returns receive a refund equal to the purchase price.

Interpretation B: The refund equals the current retail price, which may be higher or lower than what was paid.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation B an agent refunds $79.99 (current price) for an item the customer bought at $49.99 (sale price), overpaying by $30. Under Interpretation A the agent correctly refunds $49.99.

source_clause: Passengers may be reassigned to different seats or cabins during irregular operations.
ambiguity_point: The clause does not specify whether passengers involuntarily downgraded from a higher cabin class receive compensation for the fare difference.
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: Downgraded passengers receive a refund for the fare difference between their original and new cabin class.
Interpretation B: Downgraded passengers accept the new assignment with no compensation — the reassignment is within the airline's operational discretion.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation B an agent reassigns a business-class passenger to economy with no fare adjustment. Under Interpretation A the agent processes the downgrade and refunds the fare difference.

source_clause: Orders flagged by the fraud detection system require review before processing.
ambiguity_point: The clause does not specify the review timeline, who conducts the review, or what the customer should be told while their order is on hold.
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: The agent informs the customer of the hold and provides an estimated review timeline.
Interpretation B: The agent does not disclose the fraud flag and simply tells the customer the order is 'processing' without further detail.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation B an agent tells a customer 'your order is being processed' with no timeline, leaving them waiting indefinitely. Under Interpretation A the agent transparently explains the hold and gives a 24-48 hour estimate.

source_clause: Passengers may change their flight dates and times through the agent.

Interpretation B: Changes apply to all flights on the reservation, including codeshare segments operated by partner airlines.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A an agent tells a customer they cannot change their codeshare segment and must contact the partner airline. Under Interpretation B the agent changes all segments including the codeshare.

source_clause: Refunds are issued to the original payment method.
ambiguity_point: The clause does not specify how refunds are handled when the order was paid with multiple payment methods (e.g., gift card + credit card split payment).
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: The refund goes entirely to the credit card (the 'primary' payment method).
Interpretation B: The refund is split proportionally across the payment methods used.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A an agent refunds $100 entirely to the credit card, even though $40 was paid by gift card. Under Interpretation B the agent refunds $40 to the gift card and $60 to the credit card.

source_clause: Items must be in original condition for returns.
ambiguity_point: The clause does not specify what happens when an item is damaged during return shipping (in transit back to the warehouse). It is unclear whether the customer or the retailer bears the risk.
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: The customer bears the risk — if the item arrives damaged at the warehouse, the refund is reduced or denied.
Interpretation B: The retailer bears the risk — as long as the customer shipped the item in good condition, damage in transit does not affect the refund.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A an agent warns the customer that they are responsible for packaging and transit damage. Under Interpretation B the agent assures the customer that return shipping risk is covered by the retailer.

source_clause: The user needs to confirm the reason for cancellation (either 'no longer needed' or 'ordered by mistake').
ambiguity_point: 'valid reason' — valid may mean (a) one of the two enumerated acceptable reasons; (b) any reason that is logically coherent or plausible; (c) any reason the customer sincerely holds.
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: 'Valid' means from the closed list of two accepted reasons — the agent must reject any reason outside 'no longer needed' / 'ordered by mistake'.
Interpretation B: 'Valid' means any genuine, non-frivolous customer reason — the agent accepts reasons like 'found a better price' or 'changed my mind'.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation B an agent accepts 'I found a cheaper option elsewhere' and proceeds with cancellation. Under Interpretation A the agent correctly rejects this reason and asks the customer to provide one of the two permitted reasons.

annotation_rationale: Polysemy of 'valid': in everyday English 'valid reason' means any defensible reason; in a constrained policy context it means a reason from a closed enumerated set. The intended policy sense is not determinable without the enumeration.
ambiguous_clause: The agent must obtain a valid reason from the user before cancelling a pending order.
unambiguous_clause: The agent must obtain a reason from the user before cancelling a pending order, and the reason must be exactly one of: 'no longer needed' or 'ordered by mistake'. Any other stated reason is not acceptable.
user_scenario: Hi, I need to cancel order #W9842305. I found the exact same item for way cheaper on another site, so I don't need this anymore. Can you cancel it for me?

source_clause: An order can only be cancelled if its status is 'pending'.
ambiguity_point: 'not yet been processed' — processed may mean (a) the specific order status 'processed' in the system; (b) handled in any way by the warehouse or shipping system; (c) charged to the payment method.
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: 'Not yet processed' means status is not 'processed' — covers both 'pending' and potentially orders in other non-final states.
Interpretation B: 'Not yet processed' means status is specifically 'pending' — the only cancellable state.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A an agent might attempt to cancel a 'delivered' order on grounds that it is 'not processed' in the warehouse sense. Under Interpretation B only 'pending' status orders are cancellable.

annotation_rationale: Polysemy of 'processed': the word has a domain-specific meaning (a specific order status value) and a common English meaning (handled/acted upon). Without seeing the status enum, an agent may adopt the colloquial meaning.
ambiguous_clause: The agent can cancel orders that have not yet been processed.
unambiguous_clause: The agent can cancel orders whose status is exactly 'pending'. Orders with status 'processed', 'delivered', or 'cancelled' cannot be cancelled.

source_clause: The modify items action will change the order status to 'pending (items modified)' and the agent will not be able to modify or cancel the order anymore.
ambiguity_point: 'locked' and 'no further changes' — 'changes' may mean (a) only item modifications; (b) all modifications including payment; (c) any action including returns after delivery.

Interpretation B: 'No further changes' refers only to item-level modifications; payment method can still be updated.

expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A an agent accepts a gift card with $5 balance to cover a $50 price difference and calls the tool, which then fails. Under Interpretation B the agent proactively checks the gift card balance and declines if insufficient.

source_clause: You should deny user requests that are against this policy.
ambiguity_point: 'decline' — may mean (a) refuse and provide no further assistance; (b) refuse but offer alternatives; (c) mark as declined in the system; (d) deprioritize but eventually handle.
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: 'Decline' means actively refuse with an explanation and stop — no further action on the request.
Interpretation B: 'Decline' means not execute directly but still assist indirectly (e.g., transfer to human, provide information about alternative channels).
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A an agent simply says 'I cannot help with that' and ends the interaction. Under Interpretation B the agent refuses the specific action but transfers to a human or explains the alternative path.

annotation_rationale: Polysemy of 'decline': in customer service contexts, 'decline' can mean refuse-and-close, refuse-and-redirect, or deprioritize. The procedural consequence (transfer vs. terminate) depends heavily on which sense is adopted.
ambiguous_clause: Agents should decline requests that fall outside the scope of permitted retail operations.
unambiguous_clause: Agents must refuse to execute any request that is explicitly prohibited by or not covered in this policy. For requests that are not covered (i.e., out of scope), agents should transfer the user to a human agent rather than attempting to handle them.

source_clause: For a pending order, each item can be modified to an available new item of the same product but of different product option.
ambiguity_point: 'same kind' — 'kind' may mean (a) same product type (e.g., shirt vs. shirt); (b) same product with different variant options; (c) same general category (e.g., clothing); (d) same price range.
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: 'Same kind' means same general product category (e.g., any clothing item) — a shirt can be swapped for pants.
Interpretation B: 'Same kind' means same specific product type — shirt for shirt only, with different option (color, size).
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A an agent modifies a shirt to pants because both are 'clothing'. Under Interpretation B the agent correctly refuses the cross-type swap.

unambiguous_clause: Items in a pending order can be replaced with a different variant (option) of the same product type. For example, a size M blue shirt can be changed to a size L blue shirt or a size M red shirt — but not to a different product type like pants.

source_clause: At the beginning of the conversation, you have to authenticate the user identity by locating their user id via email, or via name + zip code.
ambiguity_point: 'verify identity' — may mean (a) locate user ID via email or name+zip (the policy-specified method); (b) ask for any identifying information the customer provides; (c) confirm the user's stated identity without system lookup; (d) request a password or PIN.
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: Identity verification means the agent must look up the user ID via one of two specified methods (email or name+zip), regardless of what the customer provides.
Interpretation B: Identity verification means accepting whatever identifying information the customer provides (including self-stated user IDs, order numbers, etc.).
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation B an agent accepts a customer-stated user ID without performing the email or name+zip lookup, bypassing the authentication requirement. Under Interpretation A the lookup is always required.

The policy-intended sense requires the system lookup, but the common English sense of 'verify' could allow acceptance of a provided ID.

source_clause: You can only help one user per conversation but can handle multiple requests from the same user.
ambiguity_point: 'individually' — may mean (a) one request at a time, sequentially within the same session; (b) each customer gets their own separate session; (c) each request must be handled without combining with others.
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: 'Individually' means one request per session — only a single request per conversation is permitted, even for the same user.
Interpretation B: 'Individually' means each request is addressed in turn — multiple requests from the same user are handled sequentially in one session.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A an agent ends the conversation after completing one request and requires a new session for the user's second request. Under Interpretation B the agent correctly handles the user's second request in the same session.

unambiguous_clause: The agent may handle multiple requests from the same authenticated user within a single conversation, but must not assist any other user during that conversation. If a different user needs assistance, they must start a new conversation.

annotation_rationale: Technical term polysemy: 'read-only' in database contexts means no writes are permitted but reads are still allowed. However in everyday English 'final' can imply the record is closed entirely. The operational consequence differs significantly.

source_clause: Flight can be cancelled if the booking was made within the last 24 hours.
ambiguity_point: 'recently' — may mean (a) within 24 hours of booking creation; (b) within 24 hours of departure; (c) within the same calendar day; (d) within a few days.
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: 'Recently' means within 24 hours of the booking creation timestamp.
Interpretation B: 'Recently' means within some other time window — same day, 48 hours, or within 24 hours of departure.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation B an agent cancels a booking made 30 hours ago because 'it seems recent enough.' Under Interpretation A the agent correctly enforces the 24-hour window from booking creation.

annotation_rationale: Vagueness of 'recently': the adverb lacks a defined threshold and can be interpreted as various time windows. In airline policy '24 hours from booking' is the specific rule, but 'recently' could imply same-day or other windows.
ambiguous_clause: Passengers may cancel flights booked recently without penalty.
unambiguous_clause: Passengers may cancel a flight booking made within the last 24 hours without penalty. 'Within the last 24 hours' is calculated from the current time to the time the booking was created, not from the scheduled departure time.

source_clause: It is a business flight — one of the four cancellation eligibility conditions.
ambiguity_point: 'Corporate travel bookings' and 'business travel' — 'business' may mean (a) business cabin class; (b) bookings made for corporate/work purposes; (c) bookings made through a corporate account.

Interpretation B: 'Business travel' means the purpose of the trip is work-related — any cabin class qualifies if the passenger is traveling for business.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation B an agent cancels an economy booking because the passenger claims it was for a work conference. Under Interpretation A the agent only applies the exception to actual business-cabin bookings.

source_clause: Do not proactively offer compensation unless the user explicitly asks for one.
ambiguity_point: 'raises the issue' and 'explicitly asks' — 'raises' may mean (a) directly asks using the word 'compensation'; (b) complains about the inconvenience in any form; (c) implies interest without directly requesting.

Interpretation B: 'Raises the issue' means a direct, explicit request for compensation — general complaints do not trigger the offer.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A the agent proactively offers compensation whenever a customer says 'this delay is really frustrating.' Under Interpretation B the agent only offers when the customer directly asks about compensation.

ambiguity_point: 'non-premium cabin' — may mean (a) economy only; (b) both economy and basic economy; (c) all cabins other than business.
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: 'Non-premium' covers both economy and basic economy — basic economy passengers receive economy rules.
Interpretation B: 'Non-premium' refers only to standard economy; basic economy is treated as a distinct cabin with stricter rules.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A an agent allows a basic economy passenger to modify their flight because economy-class rules permit modification. Under Interpretation B the agent correctly refuses modification for basic economy.

annotation_rationale: Vague category boundary: 'non-premium' is not a defined term in airline policy. Whether it includes only economy or also basic economy is ambiguous — and the distinction has critical operational consequences.
ambiguous_clause: Economy-class rules apply to all non-premium cabin passengers.
unambiguous_clause: Standard economy rules apply only to passengers booked in the 'economy' cabin. Basic economy is a separate cabin class with its own more restrictive rules — basic economy passengers cannot modify their reservations regardless of circumstances.
user_scenario: I have reservation RES-39201 in basic economy and I need to change my flight to a later one that same day. I know economy passengers can make changes -- basic economy is still economy class, right?

source_clause: The API does not check that cancellation rules are met, so the agent must make sure the rules apply before calling the API.
ambiguity_point: 'ensuring compliance' — may mean (a) verifying the four specific eligibility conditions before calling the tool; (b) following the conversation protocol (confirmation, documentation); (c) checking that the tool call format is correct.
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: 'Compliance' refers to process compliance (following conversation steps) — not necessarily checking the four substantive eligibility conditions.
Interpretation B: 'Compliance' means verifying the four cancellation eligibility conditions — substantive policy compliance before the tool call.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A an agent calls the cancellation tool after getting user confirmation but without checking whether any of the four eligibility conditions apply. Under Interpretation B the agent explicitly verifies eligibility before proceeding.

annotation_rationale: Polysemy of 'compliance': in procedural contexts 'compliance' can refer to process/format compliance or substantive rule compliance. The policy requires substantive eligibility checking, but the term alone does not convey this.
ambiguous_clause: Agents are responsible for ensuring compliance before executing flight cancellations.
unambiguous_clause: Agents must manually verify that at least one of the four cancellation eligibility conditions is satisfied before calling the cancel_reservation tool. The cancel_reservation tool will execute the cancellation regardless of eligibility — the agent is the only safeguard.
user_scenario: Yes, I confirm I want to cancel reservation RES-82004. Please go ahead and process it.

source_clause: The flight is cancelled by the airline — one of the four conditions under which a passenger's booking can be cancelled.
ambiguity_point: 'cancelled the service' — 'service' may mean (a) the specific flight(s) in the customer's reservation; (b) the route in general; (c) the airline's operations on that day.
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: 'Service' means the specific flights booked — only the customer's actual flights being cancelled triggers eligibility.
Interpretation B: 'Service' means the route or general service — any operational disruption on the route qualifies.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation B an agent cancels a booking because the airline announced route suspensions next month, even though the customer's specific flight is still scheduled. Under Interpretation A only an actual cancellation of the customer's flight qualifies.

annotation_rationale: Polysemy of 'service': airlines use 'service' to mean a specific flight, a route, or general operations. The policy intends the narrow sense (specific flight), but 'service' commonly refers to broader operational categories.
ambiguous_clause: Agents may cancel a reservation when the airline has cancelled the service.
unambiguous_clause: Agents may cancel a reservation when the airline has cancelled one or more of the specific flights in that reservation. A general service suspension (e.g., the airline pausing a route temporarily) that has not yet cancelled the customer's specific scheduled flight does not qualify.

source_clause: If the user complains about a cancelled flight, the agent can offer a $100-per-passenger certificate as a gesture.
ambiguity_point: 'appropriate gesture of goodwill' — may mean (a) the specific $100-per-passenger certificate; (b) any form of goodwill (apology, miles, priority boarding); (c) a variable amount at agent discretion.
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: 'Appropriate gesture' means the specifically mandated $100-per-passenger certificate — no other form is permitted.
Interpretation B: 'Appropriate gesture' allows the agent to choose any form of compensation (miles, service upgrades, variable dollar amounts) based on the situation.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation B an agent offers 500 frequent flyer miles or a $50 voucher at their discretion. Under Interpretation A the agent correctly offers only the $100-per-passenger certificate as specified.

ambiguous_clause: When a customer reports a flight disruption, the agent may offer an appropriate gesture of goodwill.
unambiguous_clause: When a customer reports that a flight in their reservation was cancelled by the airline, and the customer explicitly asks for compensation, the agent may offer a certificate of $100 per passenger in the reservation as a gesture of goodwill.

source_clause: The cabin must be consistent across all flights in the reservation when modifying.
ambiguity_point: 'matching travel class' — 'matching' and 'travel class' may mean (a) all segments in the same named cabin (economy, business, basic economy); (b) same general tier (premium vs. non-premium); (c) same fare class code.
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: 'Matching' means all flights in the exact same named cabin — economy with economy, business with business.
Interpretation B: 'Matching' means all flights in a broadly comparable tier — economy and basic economy could both count as 'matching' because they are both non-premium.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation B an agent modifies a reservation to mix economy and basic economy segments, treating them as 'matching' non-premium travel classes. Under Interpretation A the agent correctly refuses because basic economy and economy are distinct cabins.

unambiguous_clause: After modification, all flights within a reservation must be in the same cabin class (e.g., all economy or all business). Mixing cabin classes across segments (e.g., one economy leg and one business leg) is not permitted.

source_clause: Exchange delivered order: same product type constraint applies.
ambiguity_point: 'equivalent products' — 'equivalent' may mean (a) same product type with different options; (b) same price point; (c) similar functionality or category; (d) same product ID.
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: 'Equivalent' means same price range — a $30 item can be exchanged for any other $30 item regardless of type.
Interpretation B: 'Equivalent' means same product type — only exchanges within the same product category are permitted.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A an agent processes an exchange of a shirt for a pair of headphones because both are priced at $30. Under Interpretation B the agent correctly refuses and requires the exchange to stay within the same product type.

annotation_rationale: Polysemy of 'equivalent': the word can mean price-equivalent, function-equivalent, or type-equivalent. Policy intends type-equivalent (same product type), but the term without definition admits price or functional equivalence.
ambiguous_clause: Customers may exchange delivered items for equivalent products.
unambiguous_clause: Customers may exchange delivered items for a different variant (option) of the same product type. The exchanged item must be of the same product type (e.g., shirt for shirt) but can be a different size, color, or other option.

source_clause: Modify payment: the new payment method must not be the same as the existing one.
ambiguity_point: 'different from' — may mean (a) a different payment method ID (technically different record); (b) a different payment method type (e.g., can't replace credit card with another credit card); (c) functionally different in some way.
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: 'Different' means a different payment method type — cannot replace a Visa credit card with another credit card, even from a different bank.
Interpretation B: 'Different' means a different specific payment method ID — any payment method record that is not the exact same stored method qualifies.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A an agent refuses to replace one credit card with a different credit card because they are 'the same type.' Under Interpretation B the agent correctly accepts the change as long as the new card has a different ID.

annotation_rationale: Ambiguity in 'different': the comparison can be at the record level (different ID) or at the type level (different payment instrument class). The policy intends record-level difference, but 'different payment method' could imply type-level difference.
ambiguous_clause: The replacement payment method must be different from the one currently on the order.
unambiguous_clause: The replacement payment method must have a different payment method ID than the one currently associated with the order. Two gift cards are considered different payment methods if they have different IDs, even if they belong to the same user.

source_clause: You should at most make one tool call at a time and if you make a tool call you should not respond to the user at the same time.
ambiguity_point: 'complete one transaction at a time' — 'transaction' may mean (a) a single tool call per turn; (b) a complete end-to-end customer request (all related tool calls together); (c) a single database operation.
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: 'Transaction' means a complete customer request — the agent must finish all steps of the current request before starting another (no interleaving).
Interpretation B: 'Transaction' means a single tool call — the agent may initiate different requests alternately across turns, one tool call per turn.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A the agent refuses to partially address a cancellation and then start discussing a return until the cancellation is fully resolved. Under Interpretation B the agent can freely interleave tool calls for different requests across turns.

annotation_rationale: Polysemy of 'transaction': in database terminology a transaction is an atomic unit of work; in customer service it can mean a complete service episode. These map to different sequential constraints on agent behavior.
ambiguous_clause: The agent must complete one transaction at a time before initiating another.
unambiguous_clause: In each turn of the conversation, the agent must choose between either sending a message to the user or making one tool call. Both actions cannot occur in the same turn. The agent may make multiple tool calls across successive turns to complete a complex request.
