source_clause: You should not make up any information or knowledge not provided by the user or the tools.
ambiguity_point: 'can directly support' — may mean (a) information explicitly provided by user or tool; (b) information inferable from context or common knowledge; (c) information the agent is reasonably confident about.
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: 'Directly support' means provably grounded in user input or tool output — no inference or estimation.
Interpretation B: 'Directly support' means the agent believes it to be correct based on reasoning — informed inference is acceptable.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation B an agent says 'Your order will probably arrive tomorrow based on the shipping date' without querying the tracking tool. Under Interpretation A the agent only states delivery information after confirming it via a tool call.

annotation_rationale: Vagueness of 'directly support': in epistemological contexts 'supporting' evidence can mean proof (strict) or plausibility (loose). The policy intends the strict reading (explicitly provided data only), but the phrasing admits informed inference.
ambiguous_clause: Agents should only communicate information they can directly support.
unambiguous_clause: Agents should only communicate information that was explicitly provided by the customer during the conversation or returned by a tool call. Agents must not infer, estimate, or state facts not grounded in these two sources.

source_clause: If the user complains about a delayed flight and wants to change or cancel the reservation, the agent can offer a $50-per-passenger certificate.
ambiguity_point: 'significantly delayed' — 'significant' is not defined; may mean (a) any delay; (b) delay exceeding a specific threshold (e.g., 2 hours, 3 hours); (c) a delay causing a missed connection; (d) a delay the customer considers significant.
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: 'Significant' requires a minimum delay duration (e.g., 2+ hours) before compensation is warranted.
Interpretation B: Any delay — even 15 minutes — qualifies if the customer complains about it.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A an agent asks the customer how long the delay was before offering compensation, potentially denying a $50 certificate for a 45-minute delay. Under Interpretation B the agent offers compensation for any reported delay the customer complains about.

ambiguity_point: 'structure of a reservation' — 'structure' may mean (a) trip type and origin/destination only; (b) any fixed booking parameter including number of passengers or cabin; (c) the entire booking configuration.
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: 'Structure' is narrow — only trip type and origin/destination are fixed; everything else can be modified.
Interpretation B: 'Structure' is broad — any major booking parameter (passengers, cabin, trip type, routing) counts as structural and cannot be changed.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation B an agent refuses to change a cabin class or passenger count because these are 'structural.' Under Interpretation A the agent only blocks changes to trip type and origin/destination, allowing cabin and passenger modifications per other rules.

source_clause: Return delivered order: items must have been delivered (status is 'delivered').

annotation_rationale: Polysemy of 'received': the word can refer to the system-recorded delivery event (database status) or the customer's physical receipt (self-reported). These two can diverge and lead to different eligibility decisions.
ambiguous_clause: The return process applies to orders the customer has received.
unambiguous_clause: The return process applies only to orders whose status in the system is 'delivered'. Orders in transit, pending, or processed status are not eligible for return processing.

source_clause: Gold members receive 4 free checked bags in business class.
ambiguity_point: 'premium-tier' and 'upper-cabin' — 'premium-tier' may mean gold only or gold+silver; 'upper-cabin' may mean business only or business+economy (vs. basic economy).
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: 'Premium-tier' = gold only; 'upper-cabin' = business only — only gold members in business get maximum allowance.
Interpretation B: 'Premium-tier' = silver and gold; 'upper-cabin' = economy and business — silver members in economy also receive maximum allowance.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation B an agent allows a silver member booking economy to add 4 free bags. Under Interpretation A only gold-business travelers receive 4 free bags; silver members in economy get fewer.

annotation_rationale: Polysemy of category labels: 'premium-tier' and 'upper-cabin' are informal synonyms for membership and cabin tiers respectively, but their exact coverage (which tiers qualify) is ambiguous without explicit mapping to the defined tier names.
ambiguous_clause: Premium-tier travelers in upper-cabin seats receive the maximum checked baggage allowance.
unambiguous_clause: Gold-tier members booked in the business cabin receive 4 free checked bags. This is the maximum allowance in the system. Silver, regular, and all economy/basic-economy travelers receive fewer free bags according to the baggage table.
user_scenario: I'm a Silver loyalty member on reservation RES-25580 flying economy. I was told premium-tier members get free extra baggage. Silver is a premium tier, right? I'd like to add a second checked bag for free.

source_clause: You can only help one user per conversation and must deny any requests for tasks related to any other user.
ambiguity_point: 'on behalf of another party' — 'another party' may mean (a) a different user account; (b) any third party including the customer's family member at the same address; (c) a business entity; (d) anyone other than the authenticated account holder.
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: 'Another party' means any user other than the authenticated account — even a spouse making a request about their own account is refused.
Interpretation B: 'Another party' means an unauthorized third party — if the customer has authority over the other account (e.g., family manager), requests can be handled.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation B an agent helps a user manage their spouse's order because the customer claims shared account authority. Under Interpretation A the agent correctly refuses to take any action on any account other than the authenticated one.

annotation_rationale: Polysemy of 'another party': in legal contexts a 'party' is any distinct legal entity; in customer service it may connote unauthorized third parties vs. authorized representatives. The intended meaning is any other user account, but the term allows relationship-based exceptions.
ambiguous_clause: The agent must decline requests made on behalf of another party.
unambiguous_clause: The agent must refuse to perform any action (lookups, cancellations, modifications, returns) related to a user account other than the one that was authenticated at the start of the current conversation.
user_scenario: Hi, I'm calling on behalf of my spouse. They're at work and can't call right now, but they asked me to check on their order #W4412098 and cancel it if it hasn't shipped yet. Their account is under user USR-78340. They gave me full permission.

source_clause: If a delivered item arrives damaged, the agent should offer a complimentary replacement.
ambiguity_point: 'complimentary' — may mean (a) free of charge (no cost to the customer); (b) a replacement that complements or matches the original item; (c) a goodwill gesture such as an upgraded item.
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: 'Complimentary' means free — the replacement ships at no cost to the customer, same item.
Interpretation B: 'Complimentary' means a goodwill gesture — the agent may offer an upgraded or premium replacement to compensate for the inconvenience.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation B an agent offers a more expensive upgraded item as a gesture of goodwill for the damaged delivery. Under Interpretation A the agent sends the same item again at no charge, which is the intended policy.

ambiguous_clause: For damaged deliveries, the agent should arrange a complimentary replacement.
unambiguous_clause: For damaged deliveries, the agent should arrange a replacement of the exact same item at no cost to the customer. The replacement must be the same product; upgrades or substitutions are not permitted under this policy.
user_scenario: I received order #W3349021 today and the ceramic vase I ordered is cracked. It was supposed to be a gift. Can you send me a replacement? Honestly, given the hassle, it would be nice to get the premium version instead.

source_clause: Customers may modify items in a pending order before it is processed.
ambiguity_point: 'standing order' — may mean (a) a recurring/subscription order set to repeat; (b) an order that is currently active/open (standing = in effect); (c) a permanent instruction on the account.
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: 'Standing order' means a recurring subscription — cancellation stops all future deliveries.
Interpretation B: 'Standing order' means any currently active (not yet delivered) order — cancellation applies to a single order.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A an agent cancels the customer's entire subscription when they only wanted to cancel one upcoming delivery. Under Interpretation B the agent cancels just the single open order.

annotation_rationale: Polysemy of 'standing order': in banking/commerce it means a recurring instruction; in general English 'standing' can mean 'currently in effect'. The policy intends recurring subscription orders but the term allows the single-order reading.
ambiguous_clause: Customers may cancel a standing order at any time before the next shipment date.
unambiguous_clause: Customers may cancel a recurring subscription order at any time before the next scheduled shipment date. Cancellation stops all future deliveries in the subscription but does not affect orders that have already been processed or shipped.
user_scenario: Hi, I have a standing order for monthly protein powder deliveries, order #W5561190. I want to cancel the next delivery but keep the subscription going. Can you do that?

source_clause: Passengers on cancelled flights should be rebooked on the next available flight to their destination.
ambiguity_point: 'direct flight' — may mean (a) a non-stop flight with no intermediate stops; (b) a flight that does not require a plane change (may stop but same aircraft); (c) the most straightforward routing regardless of stops.
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: 'Direct' means non-stop — the agent only offers flights with zero intermediate stops.
Interpretation B: 'Direct' means no plane change — the agent offers flights that may stop at intermediate airports as long as the passenger stays on the same aircraft.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A an agent tells a passenger there are no available direct flights when a same-aircraft one-stop flight departs in 2 hours. Under Interpretation B the agent books the one-stop flight as it qualifies as 'direct.'

annotation_rationale: Domain-specific polysemy of 'direct': in aviation, 'direct' technically means same-aircraft (may include stops); in common passenger understanding, 'direct' means non-stop. The policy intends same-aircraft routing.
ambiguous_clause: When rebooking cancelled flights, agents should prioritize direct flights to the passenger's destination.
unambiguous_clause: When rebooking cancelled flights, agents should prioritize non-stop flights (no intermediate stops) to the passenger's destination. If no non-stop flight is available within 6 hours, flights with one stop but no aircraft change may be offered.
user_scenario: My flight UA-2290 from Chicago to Miami was just cancelled. I need to get there today. Can you find me a direct flight? I really don't want any stops.

source_clause: Passengers affected by significant delays should be accommodated.
ambiguity_point: 'accommodated' — may mean (a) rebooked on an alternative flight; (b) provided with hotel, meals, or ground transportation; (c) given compensation (voucher/certificate); (d) any combination of these.
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: 'Accommodated' means rebooked on the next available flight only.
Interpretation B: 'Accommodated' means provided with ground amenities (hotel, meals) in addition to rebooking.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A an agent rebooks the passenger but offers no hotel or meal vouchers for an overnight delay. Under Interpretation B the agent proactively offers hotel and meal arrangements, which is the intended policy.

ambiguous_clause: Passengers experiencing delays of 4 hours or more will be accommodated.
unambiguous_clause: Passengers experiencing delays of 4 hours or more will be rebooked on the next available flight at no additional cost. If the delay extends overnight, the airline will provide a hotel voucher, meal vouchers (up to $50 per passenger), and ground transportation to/from the hotel.

source_clause: Delivered orders may be returned if items are in original condition.
ambiguity_point: 'eligible items' — may mean (a) items that meet the return condition (original packaging, within window); (b) items in product categories that permit returns; (c) items not on the exclusion list.
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: 'Eligible' means meeting the return conditions (packaging, time window) — all product categories qualify if conditions are met.
Interpretation B: 'Eligible' means belonging to a returnable product category — some categories (e.g., perishables, digital) are excluded regardless of condition.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A an agent accepts a return of opened software because it's within the time window and in 'original condition.' Under Interpretation B the agent correctly rejects it because digital products are categorically ineligible.

ambiguous_clause: Customers may return eligible items within 30 days of delivery for a full refund.
unambiguous_clause: Customers may return items within 30 days of delivery for a full refund, provided the item is in its original unopened packaging. The following categories are excluded from returns: perishable goods, digital downloads, gift cards, and personalized/custom items.

source_clause: Agents must verify that the passenger has a confirmed reservation before making changes.
ambiguity_point: 'confirmed reservation' — may mean (a) a reservation with status 'confirmed' in the system; (b) a reservation that the passenger verbally confirms is theirs; (c) a reservation that has been paid in full (not held/pending payment).
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: 'Confirmed' means the system status field equals 'confirmed' — excludes reservations with status 'held', 'pending', or 'waitlisted'.
Interpretation B: 'Confirmed' means the passenger has confirmed their identity and the reservation belongs to them — any reservation status is acceptable.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation B an agent modifies a waitlisted reservation because the passenger confirmed it's theirs. Under Interpretation A the agent refuses because the reservation status is 'waitlisted', not 'confirmed.'

unambiguous_clause: Changes can only be made to reservations whose system status is 'confirmed'. Reservations with status 'held', 'waitlisted', 'pending payment', or 'cancelled' cannot be modified.

possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: 'Original condition' means the item is unopened and sealed — any opened product is ineligible.
Interpretation B: 'Original condition' means undamaged and complete with all accessories — the item may have been opened and inspected.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation B an agent accepts a return for headphones that were opened and tried on once. Under Interpretation A the agent rejects the return because the seal was broken.

source_clause: Compensation may be offered when passengers experience service disruptions.
ambiguity_point: 'service disruption' — may mean (a) flight cancellation or significant delay (>2 hours); (b) any deviation from scheduled service including minor delays, gate changes, or equipment substitution; (c) only events caused by the airline (not weather or ATC).
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: 'Service disruption' means cancellation or delay exceeding 2 hours caused by the airline.
Interpretation B: 'Service disruption' means any inconvenience including a 30-minute delay, seat reassignment, or catering change.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation B an agent issues compensation for a 45-minute weather delay and a gate change. Under Interpretation A the agent declines because these do not meet the threshold of a 'service disruption.'

ambiguous_clause: Passengers affected by a service disruption may receive a compensation certificate.
unambiguous_clause: Passengers whose flights were cancelled by the airline, or delayed by more than 2 hours due to airline-caused reasons (not weather, ATC, or security), may receive a compensation certificate. Minor delays, gate changes, and equipment substitutions do not qualify.

source_clause: The agent should attempt to resolve customer issues within the current interaction.
ambiguity_point: 'resolve' — may mean (a) fix the problem completely (refund, replacement, etc.); (b) address the issue by providing information or next steps; (c) ensure the customer feels heard even if no action is taken.
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: 'Resolve' means take concrete action to fix the problem — issue a refund, send a replacement, or modify the order.
Interpretation B: 'Resolve' means provide information about what the customer can do — direct them to a form, website, or different department.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation B an agent tells the customer to fill out an online return form instead of processing the return directly. Under Interpretation A the agent processes the return in the current interaction.

unambiguous_clause: The agent should take all necessary actions to fix delivery complaints during the conversation, including initiating returns, exchanges, or refunds. The agent must not redirect the customer to other channels or ask them to call back.

source_clause: Each passenger is entitled to standard baggage allowance based on their fare class.
ambiguity_point: 'standard baggage' — may mean (a) one carry-on and one personal item (cabin baggage only); (b) one checked bag plus cabin baggage; (c) the baggage included in the ticket fare, which varies by fare class.
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: 'Standard baggage' means carry-on only — checked bags always cost extra.
Interpretation B: 'Standard baggage' means one free checked bag plus carry-on — included in all fares.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation B an agent tells a basic economy passenger they have a free checked bag included. Under Interpretation A the agent correctly states that basic economy includes only a personal item and checked bags are extra.

ambiguous_clause: Passengers receive standard baggage allowance with their ticket.

source_clause: When an item is out of stock for exchange, the agent should offer a comparable alternative.
ambiguity_point: 'comparable' — may mean (a) same brand, same category, similar price; (b) same function regardless of brand or price; (c) same price point regardless of category.
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: 'Comparable' means functionally equivalent — same features, similar specs, any brand.
Interpretation B: 'Comparable' means same brand and category within ±10% of the original price.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A an agent offers a different-brand headphone at double the price because it has similar features. Under Interpretation B the agent only offers same-brand alternatives within a tight price range.

annotation_rationale: Vagueness of 'comparable': the comparison dimensions (price, brand, features, category) are unspecified. Different interpretations lead to wildly different exchange offerings.
ambiguous_clause: If the original item is unavailable, the agent may offer a comparable item for exchange.
unambiguous_clause: If the original item is unavailable, the agent may offer an alternative item from the same brand and product category, priced within 10% of the original item's price. If no such item exists, the agent must offer a refund instead.
user_scenario: I want to exchange the running shoes from order #W5510087 but I see they're out of stock. I'd accept something comparable. What do you have?

source_clause: Emergency rebooking is available for immediate family medical emergencies.
ambiguity_point: 'immediate family' — may mean (a) spouse and children only; (b) parents, siblings, spouse, and children; (c) anyone living in the same household; (d) the legal definition which varies by jurisdiction.
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: 'Immediate family' includes spouse, children, parents, and siblings.
Interpretation B: 'Immediate family' includes only spouse and dependent children.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation B an agent denies emergency rebooking for a passenger whose mother is hospitalized, because 'mother' is not spouse or dependent child. Under Interpretation A the agent approves because parents are included.

annotation_rationale: Polysemy of 'immediate family': different legal and cultural definitions exist. US IRS defines it differently from FMLA. Without explicit enumeration, agents apply inconsistent standards.
ambiguous_clause: Fee-free rebooking is available when an immediate family member has a medical emergency.
unambiguous_clause: Fee-free rebooking is available when the passenger's spouse, domestic partner, child, parent, sibling, grandparent, or grandchild has a medical emergency. The passenger must provide the family member's name and relationship. In-laws, aunts, uncles, and cousins are not covered.

source_clause: Defective products may be returned at any time regardless of the return window.
ambiguity_point: 'defective' — may mean (a) a manufacturing defect present at time of purchase; (b) any malfunction including wear-and-tear damage; (c) not performing as the customer expected (perceived defect).
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: 'Defective' means a manufacturing or design flaw — the product was faulty when sold.
Interpretation B: 'Defective' means any product that does not work, including damage from normal use over time.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation B an agent accepts a return for a phone charger that stopped working after 8 months of daily use (wear and tear). Under Interpretation A the agent only accepts returns for products with demonstrable manufacturing flaws.

annotation_rationale: Vagueness of 'defective': in consumer law it refers to manufacturing defects; in common usage customers call any broken product 'defective.' The policy intends manufacturing defects but the term invites broader claims.
ambiguous_clause: Defective items may be returned outside the standard return window for a full refund.
unambiguous_clause: Items with a verifiable manufacturing defect (a flaw in materials or workmanship present at the time of sale) may be returned outside the standard 30-day return window for a full refund. Damage from normal wear and tear, misuse, or accident is not considered a defect.
user_scenario: I bought a laptop bag from order #W2214530 about 4 months ago. The zipper broke yesterday. I think it's defective — can I return it even though it's past 30 days?

source_clause: Passengers must present valid travel documents at check-in.
ambiguity_point: 'travel document' — may mean (a) passport only; (b) passport or government-issued photo ID; (c) any document sufficient for travel including visa, boarding pass, or itinerary confirmation.
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: 'Travel document' means a government-issued photo ID — driver's license or passport for domestic, passport for international.
Interpretation B: 'Travel document' means booking confirmation or itinerary — the reservation printout.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation B an agent accepts a printed itinerary as sufficient documentation for check-in. Under Interpretation A the agent requires government-issued photo ID and denies boarding without it.

Interpretation B: 'Store credit' means a one-time discount code for the next purchase only — expires after use or a set period.

source_clause: Changes to non-refundable tickets are subject to a penalty.
ambiguity_point: 'penalty' — may mean (a) a fixed change fee (e.g., $200); (b) forfeiture of the ticket value; (c) the fare difference between old and new tickets; (d) a combination of change fee plus fare difference.
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: 'Penalty' means a flat change fee — the passenger pays a fixed amount regardless of fare difference.
Interpretation B: 'Penalty' means the passenger forfeits the original ticket value and must buy a new ticket at full price.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation B an agent tells the passenger they lose their $400 ticket and must buy a new one. Under Interpretation A the agent charges a $200 change fee plus any fare difference, preserving the original ticket value.

ambiguous_clause: Modifications to non-refundable bookings incur a penalty.
unambiguous_clause: Modifications to non-refundable bookings incur a change fee of $200 per passenger per direction. The passenger must also pay any fare difference between the original and new ticket. The original ticket value is preserved (not forfeited).
user_scenario: I have a non-refundable ticket on reservation RES-90210 and I need to change my travel date. What's the penalty? Am I losing the whole ticket value or just paying a fee?

expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation B an agent silently modifies the order and relies on an automated email. Under Interpretation A the agent explicitly tells the customer what will change and gets acknowledgment before proceeding.

source_clause: Compensation certificates do not cover incidental expenses.
ambiguity_point: 'incidental expenses' — may mean (a) minor expenses like snacks and phone calls; (b) all non-ticket expenses including hotel and ground transport; (c) expenses not directly caused by the disruption.
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: 'Incidental' means small convenience expenses — compensation covers hotel and meals but not phone calls or magazines.
Interpretation B: 'Incidental' means all non-flight expenses — compensation covers only the ticket value, not hotel, meals, or transport.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation B an agent tells a stranded overnight passenger that the certificate only covers the flight rebooking, not their hotel. Under Interpretation A the agent correctly covers hotel and meals as non-incidental necessities.

possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: 'Premium' means customers enrolled in the paid Premium membership program.
Interpretation B: 'Premium' means high-spending customers regardless of membership tier — anyone who spent over $1000 in the past year.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation B an agent gives enhanced support (extended return window, priority queue) to a high-spender who is on the free Standard tier. Under Interpretation A the agent correctly restricts enhanced support to enrolled Premium members only.

expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation B an agent charges a reduced $75 change fee instead of the standard $200, telling the passenger the fee was 'waived' (partially). Under Interpretation A the agent charges $0, fully eliminating the fee.

source_clause: An order can only be cancelled if its status is 'pending', and the user must confirm the reason for cancellation.
ambiguity_point: 'must not ... all' — negation wide-scope: agents must not cancel any order (blanket prohibition); negation narrow-scope: agents need not cancel every order (selectivity permitted).
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A (not > all): Agents are prohibited from cancelling any pending orders without confirmation — a blanket requirement for confirmation on every order.
Interpretation B (all > not): Agents are not required to cancel all pending orders without confirmation — some orders can be cancelled without confirmation, just not all of them.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation B an agent might cancel some orders unilaterally without confirmation, treating the rule as permission to skip confirmation on individual cases. Under Interpretation A the agent always requires confirmation before any cancellation.

annotation_rationale: Classic negation–universal quantifier scope ambiguity: 'must not cancel all' is ambiguous between NOT(∀x cancel(x)) and ∀x NOT(cancel(x) without confirmation). The two readings yield opposite behavioral prescriptions for individual cancellation events.
ambiguous_clause: Agents must not cancel all pending orders without user confirmation.
unambiguous_clause: Agents must not cancel any pending order without first obtaining explicit user confirmation; every cancellation requires confirmation.
user_scenario: Hey, I need to cancel all three of my pending orders — ORD-88412, ORD-88413, and ORD-88414. I ordered a bunch of stuff last night and changed my mind on everything. Can you just cancel them all at once?

source_clause: The user needs to confirm the order id and the reason (either 'no longer needed' or 'ordered by mistake') for cancellation.
ambiguity_point: 'different product option or a different product type' — does 'different' scope over just 'product option' (narrow: option OR type are both valid targets) or does the disjunction compound two separately scoped alternatives?
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: Modification is allowed to either a different option of the same product OR to a completely different product type — cross-type modification is permitted.
Interpretation B: Modification is allowed to a different option within the same product type only; 'product type' modification is a separate (impermissible) class.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A the agent approves a customer switching from a blue shirt to black shoes (cross-type). Under Interpretation B the agent refuses this and only allows e.g. blue shirt → red shirt.

annotation_rationale: Coordination ambiguity: the disjunction 'different product option or a different product type' allows two parses — one in which both alternatives are valid modification targets, and one in which only same-type option changes are valid. Different scope assignments produce categorically different permission sets.
ambiguous_clause: Every item in a pending order may be modified once to a different product option or a different product type.
unambiguous_clause: Every item in a pending order may be modified once to a different option of the same product type only; changing to a different product type is not permitted.
user_scenario: I have a pending order ORD-77201 for a men's blue cotton shirt, size L. I actually don't want a shirt anymore — can you change it to a pair of running shoes instead? Something in size 10, black if possible.

source_clause: Before taking any action that updates the database, you must list the action details and obtain explicit user confirmation.
ambiguity_point: 'before updating the database or transferring to a human agent' — does 'before' scope over the full disjunction (confirmation required before either action) or only over 'updating the database'?
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: The confirmation requirement applies to both database updates AND transfers to a human agent — the agent must list details and confirm before transferring.
Interpretation B: The confirmation requirement applies only to database updates; transfer to a human agent can happen without prior listing and confirmation.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A the agent delays a transfer while listing and confirming details, potentially frustrating a user who needs urgent human assistance. Under Interpretation B transfers happen immediately when warranted.

annotation_rationale: Conditional–disjunction scope ambiguity: 'before [X or Y]' is ambiguous between 'before X and before Y' (wide scope of 'before') and 'before X, or Y' (narrow scope). Two distinct behavioral prescriptions result.
ambiguous_clause: You must list and confirm all action details before updating the database or transferring to a human agent.
unambiguous_clause: Before updating the database, you must list all action details and obtain explicit confirmation. Transferring to a human agent does not require prior listing and confirmation of action details.
user_scenario: Look, I've been dealing with this damaged product issue for 20 minutes now and I just want to talk to a real person. Can you transfer me to a human agent right away? I don't want to go through any more steps.

source_clause: Refunds are processed via the original payment method immediately if it is a gift card, otherwise in 5 to 7 business days.
ambiguity_point: 'cancelled or returned orders' — does the 'immediately if gift card' condition apply to both order types (wide scope) or only to cancellations (narrow scope)?
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: Immediate gift-card refunds apply to both cancelled orders and returned orders — any gift card refund across both operations is immediate.
Interpretation B: Immediate gift-card refunds apply only to cancellations; returns follow a different timeline regardless of payment method.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A the agent tells a customer returning an order that their gift card refund will be immediate. Under Interpretation B the agent correctly states a 5–7 business day timeline for returns even when the method is a gift card.

annotation_rationale: Quantifier–conditional scope ambiguity: the disjunctive subject 'cancelled or returned orders' interacts with the conditional 'if it is a gift card' in a way that allows the conditional to scope over either just one or both order types.
ambiguous_clause: Refunds for cancelled or returned orders are processed immediately via the original payment method if it is a gift card.
unambiguous_clause: Refunds for cancelled orders are processed immediately via the original payment method if it is a gift card. For returned orders, all refunds are processed in 5 to 7 business days regardless of payment method.
user_scenario: I need to return order ORD-55120 — the blender I got is defective. I paid for it with a gift card. How long will it take for the refund to show up back on my gift card?

source_clause: You can only help one user per conversation and must deny any requests for tasks related to any other user.
ambiguity_point: 'authenticating each request' — does 'each' scope such that every individual request requires re-authentication (distributive), or does authentication of the session cover all subsequent requests (collective)?
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A (distributive): Every individual request requires its own authentication step — the agent must re-verify the user's identity before each action.
Interpretation B (collective): A single authentication at the start of the conversation covers all requests in that session.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A the agent repeatedly asks for email/zip verification before each tool call, creating severe friction and unusual behavior. Under Interpretation B authentication happens once and all subsequent requests proceed without re-verification.

annotation_rationale: Distributive–collective quantifier ambiguity on 'each request': the distributive reading requires per-request authentication while the collective reading treats the conversation session as the unit. These produce radically different interaction patterns.
ambiguous_clause: Agents may not assist users with orders or account changes without authenticating each request.
unambiguous_clause: Agents may not assist any user with orders or account changes until that user has been authenticated at the start of the conversation. Authentication is performed once per conversation, not per request.
user_scenario: Hi, it's me again — I already verified my identity at the start of this chat. I need you to look up my email sarah.l@email.com, then also find my account by name and zip — Sarah Lawson, 90210. I have questions about two different orders.

source_clause: For a pending order, each item can be modified to an available new item of the same product but of different product option.
ambiguity_point: 'before modifying items or payment methods' — does 'confirm details and payment' scope over both operations jointly or does each operation have its own confirmation requirements?
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: A single confirmation covering both details and payment is required before either operation — the agent asks for one combined confirmation.
Interpretation B: Each operation type (item modification vs. payment modification) has its own confirmation requirement with its own relevant details to confirm.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A the agent might present a combined confirmation for both item and payment changes even when only one is requested. Under Interpretation B the agent tailors confirmation content to the specific operation being performed.

annotation_rationale: Modal–disjunction scope ambiguity: the obligation 'must confirm' interacts with the disjunctive object 'items or payment methods' such that the scope of the confirmation requirement over each disjunct is unclear.
ambiguous_clause: The agent must confirm details and payment before modifying items or payment methods in a pending order.
unambiguous_clause: Before modifying items in a pending order, the agent must confirm the item details and the payment method for price differences. Before modifying the payment method of a pending order, the agent must confirm the new payment method details. Both operations require separate explicit confirmations.

source_clause: Flight can be cancelled if any of the following is true: the booking was made within the last 24 hours, the flight was cancelled by the airline, it is a business flight, or the user has travel insurance and the cancellation reason is covered.
ambiguity_point: 'travel insurance and the booking was made within 24 hours or the airline cancelled' — does 'travel insurance' conjoin only with '24 hours' (narrow) or with the entire disjunction including 'airline cancelled' (wide)?
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A (insurance AND (24h OR airline-cancelled)): Travel insurance is required for both the 24-hour window and airline-cancellation cases.
Interpretation B ((insurance AND 24h) OR airline-cancelled): Airline cancellation is a standalone condition that does not require insurance; insurance is only relevant for the 24-hour case.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A the agent refuses to cancel a flight that was cancelled by the airline if the user lacks travel insurance — an incorrect denial. Under Interpretation B the agent correctly processes the cancellation because airline cancellation is a standalone qualifying condition.

function.name: cancel_reservation

annotation_rationale: Conjunction–disjunction scope ambiguity: 'A and B or C' is canonically ambiguous between '(A and B) or C' and 'A and (B or C)', producing structurally distinct eligibility conditions.
ambiguous_clause: Agents may cancel a flight if the user has travel insurance and the booking was made within 24 hours or the flight was cancelled by the airline.
unambiguous_clause: Agents may cancel a flight if: (a) the user has travel insurance AND the cancellation reason is covered by insurance, OR (b) the booking was made within 24 hours, OR (c) the flight was cancelled by the airline. Conditions (b) and (c) do not require travel insurance.
user_scenario: My flight from JFK to LAX on May 25th was just cancelled by the airline. I booked it three days ago and I do have travel insurance on the reservation. I'd like a full refund. My reservation is RES-40291.

function.name: cancel_reservation
function.description: Cancel a flight reservation. The agent must verify that at least one cancellation eligibility condition is met before calling this tool. The tool does not perform eligibility checks.

source_clause: Basic economy is a cabin class completely distinct from economy and cannot be modified once booked.
ambiguity_point: 'basic economy tickets or bookings made more than 24 hours ago' — does 'passengers with' scope over both disjuncts (two independent conditions) or only over 'basic economy tickets' (modifier attachment to first disjunct only)?
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: Two independent conditions: (1) basic economy passengers cannot modify, AND (2) anyone whose booking is over 24 hours old cannot modify — regardless of cabin.
Interpretation B: A single compound condition: passengers who have basic economy tickets or have made bookings over 24 hours ago — both attributes must characterize the same passenger type.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A a non-basic-economy passenger with a booking over 24 hours old is blocked from modification. Under Interpretation B only basic-economy passengers with old bookings are blocked, allowing non-basic-economy passengers with older bookings to modify.

annotation_rationale: Modifier attachment ambiguity in coordination: 'passengers with [X or Y]' allows 'with' to scope over just X or over the entire disjunction X or Y, producing different eligibility sets.
ambiguous_clause: Passengers with basic economy tickets or bookings made more than 24 hours ago cannot modify their flights.
unambiguous_clause: The following passengers cannot modify their flights: (a) any passenger holding a basic economy ticket, regardless of when the booking was made; (b) any passenger — in any cabin class — whose booking was made more than 24 hours ago.
user_scenario: I have reservation RES-51123 for a flight on June 3rd. It's a regular economy ticket, not basic economy. I booked it about two days ago. I need to change my flight to June 5th instead — is that possible?

source_clause: Do not proactively offer compensation unless the user explicitly asks for one.
ambiguity_point: 'must not proactively offer all eligible passengers compensation' — 'not ... all' scope: (a) agents must not offer compensation to any passenger proactively; (b) agents need not offer compensation to every eligible passenger, but may offer it to some.
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A (not > all): No proactive compensation offers to any passenger — agents wait for explicit requests.
Interpretation B (all > not): Agents are not required to offer compensation to all passengers — selective proactive offers to some passengers are acceptable.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation B an agent might proactively inform some passengers about compensation eligibility while skipping others. Under Interpretation A the agent never proactively mentions compensation to anyone, which is the intended behavior.

annotation_rationale: Classic 'not … all' scope ambiguity: the negation can take wide scope (prohibiting all proactive offers) or narrow scope (prohibiting only complete coverage), yielding materially different compliance behaviors.
ambiguous_clause: Agents must not proactively offer all eligible passengers compensation for flight delays.
unambiguous_clause: Agents must not proactively offer compensation for flight delays to any eligible passenger; compensation is offered only when a passenger explicitly requests it.

source_clause: The cabin must be consistent across all flights in the reservation when modifying.
ambiguity_point: 'may upgrade to business class or change connecting flight legs' — are these two independent permitted modifications, or does 'upgrade to business class' scope as a sub-type of 'change connecting flight legs'?
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: Two independent permitted modifications — agents may either upgrade cabin class OR change flight legs as separate operations.
Interpretation B: Only leg changes are permitted; 'upgrade to business' is listed as a type of change, meaning upgrades are only possible in the context of a leg change.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A an agent processes a standalone business class upgrade. Under Interpretation B the agent refuses an upgrade-only request and requires a flight-leg change to accompany any cabin change.

annotation_rationale: Coordination ambiguity: 'A or B' with disjuncts of different action types creates ambiguity about whether both are independently permitted or B is a subspecification of A.
ambiguous_clause: Agents may upgrade passengers to business class or change connecting flight legs when modifying a reservation.
unambiguous_clause: When modifying a reservation, agents may change connecting flight legs, but the cabin class must remain the same across all flights; upgrading to business class requires a separate process and cannot be combined with a leg change in the same modification.
user_scenario: I'd like to upgrade my seat on reservation RES-71034 from economy to business class. I don't want to change any of my flight legs — same flights, same dates, just a cabin upgrade. How much would that cost?

source_clause: Only compensate if the user is a silver/gold member, has travel insurance, or flies business class.
ambiguity_point: 'silver or gold members with travel insurance who fly business class' — are these three independent criteria joined by OR, or a single conjunctive profile requiring all three?
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A (conjunctive): All three attributes must be present — only a silver/gold member who also has insurance AND flies business is eligible.
Interpretation B (disjunctive): Any one attribute is sufficient — silver/gold membership, insurance, or business class each independently qualifies.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A the agent denies compensation to a gold member in economy with no insurance. Under Interpretation B the agent correctly approves compensation based on gold membership alone.

annotation_rationale: Quantifier–coordination scope ambiguity: the phrase 'members with insurance who fly business' can be parsed as A AND B AND C (conjunctive) or as three independently sufficient conditions (disjunctive), with drastically different eligibility outcomes.
ambiguous_clause: Only compensate silver or gold members with travel insurance who fly business class.
unambiguous_clause: Compensate a user if any one of the following is true: (a) the user is a silver or gold member, (b) the user has travel insurance, or (c) the user flies in the business cabin. Any single condition suffices; they are independent.
user_scenario: I'm a Gold member and my flight UA-889 from Chicago to Miami was cancelled by the airline. I was booked in economy, and I don't have travel insurance on this reservation. Reservation RES-82001. Am I eligible for any compensation or travel certificate?

source_clause: Each extra checked bag beyond the free allowance costs $50.
ambiguity_point: 'gold and silver members may check additional bags' — does 'additional' scope per-member-tier (each tier has its own distinct additional allowance) or collectively (gold and silver together share an undifferentiated additional allowance)?
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A (collective): Gold and silver members share the same undifferentiated 'additional bags at no charge' benefit without tier-specific quantities.
Interpretation B (distributive): Each tier (gold, silver) has its own specific additional allowance, with gold receiving more than silver.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A the agent cannot determine how many free additional bags a silver vs. gold member gets and applies the same (possibly incorrect) allowance to both. Under Interpretation B the agent correctly applies tier-specific allowances.

annotation_rationale: Collective–distributive quantifier ambiguity: 'gold and silver members' as a collective subject creates ambiguity about whether the benefit applies uniformly to both (collective) or differentially per tier (distributive).
ambiguous_clause: Gold and silver members may check additional bags beyond the standard allowance at no charge.
unambiguous_clause: Silver members receive one additional free checked bag beyond the standard allowance. Gold members receive two additional free checked bags beyond the standard allowance. Any bags beyond these extended allowances cost $50 each.
user_scenario: Hi, I'm a Silver tier member. I'm flying from Boston to Seattle next week on reservation RES-93045. How many checked bags can I bring for free? I want to add my bags now.

source_clause: If the user complains about a delayed flight and wants to change or cancel the reservation, the agent can offer a $50-per-passenger certificate.
ambiguity_point: 'if a flight is delayed and the user requests a change or cancellation' — does 'change or cancellation' scope as a single disjunctive request-type, or can the compensation be offered for either a delay report alone OR a change/cancel request alone?
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A (both conditions required): Compensation requires both a delayed flight AND a change/cancel request — neither alone qualifies.
Interpretation B (either condition sufficient): Compensation can be offered for a delay complaint alone, or for a change/cancel request alone, without requiring both.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation B an agent offers a certificate whenever a user requests any flight change, even without a delay. Under Interpretation A the agent correctly requires both the delay and the change/cancel request.

annotation_rationale: Conditional–conjunction scope: 'if A and B' is read as requiring both conditions, but surface structure allows parsing where 'and' conjoins two independently sufficient triggers rather than jointly necessary conditions.
ambiguous_clause: Agents may offer compensation if a flight is delayed and the user requests a change or cancellation.
unambiguous_clause: Agents may offer a $50-per-passenger certificate only if both conditions are met: (1) a flight in the reservation is delayed, AND (2) the user explicitly requests to change or cancel the reservation as a result. The agent must first process the change or cancellation, then offer the certificate.

source_clause: A reservation can include at most 5 passengers.
ambiguity_point: 'up to the maximum allowed' — does the maximum scope over the total reservation (one cap for all flights combined) or per flight segment (each leg has its own cap of up to 5)?
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A (per reservation): The 5-passenger cap applies to the entire reservation — at most 5 passengers across all segments.
Interpretation B (per segment): Each flight segment can have up to 5 passengers independently, allowing more than 5 unique passengers across a multi-leg itinerary.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation B an agent books a 3-leg trip with 4 passengers on each segment as different travelers, totaling 12 distinct passengers, without triggering the cap. Under Interpretation A 5 passengers total is the hard limit.

ambiguous_clause: Agents may book any number of passengers on connected flights up to the maximum allowed.
unambiguous_clause: Agents may book up to 5 passengers total on a reservation, regardless of how many flight segments the reservation includes. The 5-passenger limit applies to the reservation as a whole, not per flight segment.

source_clause: You should not make up any information or knowledge not provided by the user or the tools.
ambiguity_point: 'all order and product information' — does 'all' distribute over both 'order information' and 'product information' independently, or does it scope only over 'order information' with 'product information' as a separate (possibly optional) check?
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A (all distributes): Agents must verify all information of both types — comprehensive verification of both order and product details is required before any action.
Interpretation B (all scopes narrowly): 'All order information' must be verified; product information verification is a separate, optional step.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation B an agent might cancel an order without checking current product availability or pricing, missing relevant product-level constraints. Under Interpretation A both checks are required.

annotation_rationale: Universal quantifier scope in NP-coordination: 'all [A and B]' is ambiguous between ∀(A) ∧ ∀(B) (all of each type) and ∀(A), B (all of A type, B mentioned separately). Different verification requirements result.
ambiguous_clause: Agents must verify all order and product information before modifying or cancelling any pending order.
unambiguous_clause: Before modifying or cancelling a pending order, agents must verify both the order information (status, items, payment) and any product information relevant to the modification by using the available lookup tools.

source_clause: The modify items action can only be called once and will change the order status to 'pending (items modified)'.
ambiguity_point: 'more than once in pending orders with multiple items' — does the modification limit scope per-order (one modification per order) or per-item (one modification per individual item within an order)?
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A (per order): The entire order can only have its items modified once total — all item changes must be submitted in a single call.
Interpretation B (per item): Each individual item within an order can be modified at most once — multiple calls are allowed as long as each item is only touched once.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation B an agent allows a customer to call modify-items three times on a 3-item order (once per item). Under Interpretation A the agent correctly enforces a single modification call regardless of item count.

annotation_rationale: Quantifier scope over granularity level: 'more than once' can scope over the order entity or over each item entity within the order, producing different enforcement of the one-time limit.
ambiguous_clause: An agent may not modify items more than once in pending orders with multiple items.
unambiguous_clause: An agent may modify the items of any pending order at most once, regardless of how many items the order contains. The one-time limit applies to the entire modify-items action, not per item.

source_clause: The total will be refunded via the original payment method immediately if it is a gift card, otherwise in 5 to 7 business days.
ambiguity_point: 'exchanges and returns … refunded to the original payment method or a new gift card' — does 'or a new gift card' apply to both exchanges and returns, or only to returns?
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: Both exchanges and returns can be refunded to either the original payment method or a new gift card.
Interpretation B: Only returns can be directed to a new gift card; exchanges use only the original payment method.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A the agent offers a gift card as a refund option when processing an exchange. Under Interpretation B the agent only offers the original payment method for exchanges.

annotation_rationale: Scope of disjunctive complement over conjoined subject: 'exchanges and returns [refunded to A or B]' is ambiguous about whether the B option (new gift card) applies to both subjects or only the nearer one (returns).
ambiguous_clause: Exchanges and returns on delivered orders are refunded to the original payment method or a new gift card.
unambiguous_clause: For returned delivered orders, refunds go to the original payment method or to an existing gift card (customer's choice). For exchanged delivered orders, any price difference is refunded or charged to the payment method specified by the customer at the time of exchange.

source_clause: You must deny any requests for tasks related to any other user.
ambiguity_point: 'about every user' — does 'not … every' mean (a) agents must not share information about any user at all, or (b) agents need not share information about every user, implying selective sharing is permitted?
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A (not > every): Agents are prohibited from sharing information about any user — complete information blackout.
Interpretation B (every > not): Agents are not required to share information about every user — they may choose which users' info to share.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A the agent even refuses to share the authenticated user's own order history. Under Interpretation B the agent shares current user info freely but might share other users' info selectively — a privacy violation.

annotation_rationale: Negation–universal quantifier scope ambiguity in restriction context: NOT(∀x share(x)) vs. ∀x NOT(share(x) for other-users). Both readings are syntactically valid but produce opposite privacy enforcement behaviors.
ambiguous_clause: Agents must not share order details or profile information about every user in the system.
unambiguous_clause: Agents must not share the order details or profile information of any user other than the currently authenticated user; the current user's own information may be shared freely.

source_clause: If any portion of the flight has already been flown, the agent cannot help and a transfer is needed.
ambiguity_point: 'any flight segment or passenger has already departed' — does the restriction trigger when any segment is flown OR when any individual passenger has departed, creating two independently sufficient triggers?
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: Two independent triggers — if any segment is departed OR if any individual passenger has departed, modification/cancellation is blocked.
Interpretation B: A compound trigger — the segment and passenger criteria are synonymous descriptions of the same condition (a segment departure).
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A an agent refuses to process changes when even one passenger on a multi-passenger booking has checked in and departed while others have not. Under Interpretation B the restriction applies at the segment level only.

ambiguous_clause: Agents may not modify or cancel reservations where any flight segment or passenger has already departed.
unambiguous_clause: Agents may not modify or cancel a reservation if any flight segment within that reservation has already been flown. Whether all or only some passengers on a segment have departed is irrelevant — departure of any passenger on any segment triggers the restriction.

source_clause: Payment for a booking may include at most one travel certificate, one credit card, and three gift cards.
ambiguity_point: 'every passenger … may pay using one certificate or one credit card' — does 'every passenger' distribute (each passenger has their own payment method allowance) or does it collectively describe the booking's payment structure?
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A (distributive): Each passenger may individually provide one certificate or one credit card — allowing up to 5 certificates in a 5-passenger booking.
Interpretation B (collective): The booking as a whole is limited to one certificate or one credit card total, shared across all passengers.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A an agent accepts 3 certificates for a 3-passenger booking. Under Interpretation B the agent correctly limits the booking to one certificate or one credit card total.

source_clause: You should transfer the user to a human agent if and only if the request cannot be handled within the scope of your actions.
ambiguity_point: 'should not attempt to resolve all complaints before transferring' — wide-scope negation: agents should never attempt to resolve any complaint before transferring; narrow-scope: agents need not attempt to resolve every complaint before transferring (some can transfer directly).
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A (not > all): Agents should not attempt to resolve any complaint prior to transfer — transfer immediately for all issues.
Interpretation B (all > not): Agents are not obligated to attempt resolution of every complaint — selective resolution before transfer is appropriate.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A agents transfer users for every complaint without attempting resolution, even for clearly in-scope requests. Under Interpretation B agents handle in-scope requests themselves and only transfer out-of-scope ones.

annotation_rationale: Negation–universal scope over procedure: NOT(∀x attempt(x) before transfer) vs. ∀x NOT(must-attempt(x)) before transfer. Wide-scope negation prohibits all pre-transfer attempts; narrow-scope merely removes a blanket requirement.
ambiguous_clause: Agents should not attempt to resolve all complaints before transferring to a human agent.
unambiguous_clause: Agents are not required to attempt resolution of every complaint before transferring; they should transfer immediately when a request is clearly out of scope, without first attempting to handle it.

source_clause: Once the user has been authenticated, you can provide information about their order, product, and profile.
ambiguity_point: 'to authenticated users and new callers' — does the permission ('may provide product information or order details') scope over both recipient groups, or does each information type apply to a different recipient group?
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: Both product information and order details may be provided to both authenticated users and new unauthenticated callers.
Interpretation B: Product information may be provided to new callers, but order details require authentication — the two information types apply to different caller categories.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A an agent shares a user's order history with an unauthenticated caller. Under Interpretation B the agent correctly restricts order details to authenticated users only.

source_clause: Confirm the facts before offering compensation.
ambiguity_point: 'confirm all flight details and passenger counts before processing any changes or offering compensation' — does a single combined confirmation satisfy requirements for both operations, or does each operation require its own dedicated confirmation step?
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: One combined confirmation of all details covers both flight changes and compensation offers.
Interpretation B: Two distinct confirmation steps are required — one for flight changes and a separate one specifically for compensation eligibility.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A an agent confirms details once and then both modifies a flight and offers compensation in a single step. Under Interpretation B the agent must separately confirm compensation-specific facts before issuing a certificate.

source_clause: The origin and destination of the trip cannot be changed when modifying a flight.
ambiguity_point: 'cannot change the origin or destination for any passenger or flight segment' — does 'cannot change' scope over passengers and segments as separate dimensions of prohibition, or does 'any passenger or flight segment' form a single compound domain of application?
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: Two separate prohibitions — (1) cannot change origin/destination for any passenger, AND (2) cannot change origin/destination for any flight segment.
Interpretation B: A single prohibition applying to the trip entity: the trip's origin and destination (as defined at booking) cannot be changed, period.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A an agent blocks any change that touches origin/destination at any level of granularity. Under Interpretation B the agent might allow renaming an intermediate stop if the overall trip origin and destination remain unchanged.

ambiguity_point: 'modify each item … once or process an exchange' — does 'of the same product type' scope over just 'exchange' or over both 'modify each item' and 'exchange'?

Interpretation B (wide scope): 'Same product type' constrains both modifications and exchanges — both operations must stay within the same product type.

source_clause: It is a business flight — one of the four conditions under which flight cancellation is permitted.
ambiguity_point: 'all business and basic economy passengers' — does 'all' distribute separately (all business may cancel AND all basic economy may cancel) or does it describe a combined group differentiated from other passengers?
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A (distributive): Both business passengers and basic economy passengers individually qualify for cancellation through the corporate portal.
Interpretation B (collective group): 'Business and basic economy' describes a collective passenger category distinct from standard economy, and only business within this group may cancel.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A an agent cancels a basic economy ticket because the customer booked through the corporate portal. Under Interpretation B the agent correctly refuses — basic economy retains its no-cancellation rule.

ambiguity_point: 'collect a payment method when modifying items or processing returns' — does the same payment-method collection apply identically to both operations, or does each operation have its own payment-information requirement?

Interpretation B: Modifications require collecting a payment method for price differences; returns require collecting a refund destination — different information is needed for each.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A an agent asks for a new payment method when processing a return even when the customer just wants a refund to the original method. Under Interpretation B the agent asks the correct question for each operation type.

source_clause: Do not compensate if the user is a regular member and has no travel insurance and flies (basic) economy.
ambiguity_point: 'regular members without travel insurance or basic economy passengers' — does the exclusion apply to (regular members without insurance) OR (basic economy passengers) as two independent groups, or to (regular members) who are (without insurance or in basic economy)?
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: Two separate excluded groups — regular members without insurance are excluded, AND basic economy passengers are excluded (regardless of membership).
Interpretation B: One conjunctive exclusion — users who are regular members AND (lack insurance OR fly basic economy) are excluded.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A a gold member flying basic economy is denied compensation because they fall in the 'basic economy passengers' excluded group. Under Interpretation B a gold member flying basic economy is still eligible because they are not a 'regular member'.

Interpretation B: Post-confirmation procedures differ by operation type — cancellations only need status update while modifications need status update plus detail confirmation.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A the agent sends an explicit notification message after both cancellations and modifications. Under Interpretation B the agent applies operation-specific post-confirmation behavior.

ambiguous_clause: After confirming any pending cancellation or modification, agents must update the order status and notify the user.
unambiguous_clause: After confirming a cancellation, agents must call the cancel tool to update the order status; the system will automatically refund the user — no separate notification is needed. source_clause: The cabin cannot be changed if any flight in the reservation has already been flown.
ambiguity_point: 'may not modify cabin or change flight legs if any passenger has checked in' — does 'if any passenger has checked in' scope as the trigger for both prohibitions (cabin change AND leg change) or only for leg changes?
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A: Check-in is the trigger for both restrictions — any checked-in passenger blocks both cabin changes and leg changes.
Interpretation B: The check-in trigger applies only to leg changes; cabin changes are blocked by a different condition (any segment flown), not by check-in.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A an agent refuses to change a cabin even when no flights have been flown but a passenger has checked in — potentially incorrect. Under Interpretation B the agent applies the appropriate trigger for each restriction type.

source_clause: Exchanges are permitted on delivered orders, but customers may request a refund or a replacement for defective items.
ambiguity_point: 'may request a refund or a replacement for defective items' — does 'for defective items' modify only 'replacement' (narrow attachment) or the entire disjunction 'refund or replacement' (wide attachment)?
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A (wide attachment: defective scopes over disjunction): Both refunds and replacements are only available for defective items — non-defective items cannot be refunded or replaced.
Interpretation B (narrow attachment: defective scopes over replacement only): Refunds are available for any delivered item, but replacements are restricted to defective items.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A the agent refuses a refund for a non-defective item the customer simply dislikes. Under Interpretation B the agent processes the refund because the defective-item restriction only applies to replacements.

unambiguous_clause: Customers may request a refund for any item in a delivered order, regardless of condition. Replacements are available only for items that are defective.

source_clause: Agents may modify the payment method or items in each pending order once.
ambiguity_point: 'may modify the payment method or items ... once' — does 'once' scope over the disjunction (one modification total, either payment or items) or over each disjunct independently (one payment change AND one item change)?
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A (once > disjunction): Each pending order gets exactly one modification action total — if the agent modifies the payment method, items cannot also be modified.
Interpretation B (once per disjunct): Each pending order may have both one payment modification and one item modification — 'once' applies independently to each action type.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A the agent refuses to modify items after already changing the payment method on the same order. Under Interpretation B the agent allows both modifications since each action type is used at most once.

ambiguous_clause: Agents may modify the payment method or items in each pending order once.
unambiguous_clause: Each pending order may be modified at most once in total. The single modification may be either a payment method change or an item change, but not both.

source_clause: Agents should not process returns on all orders that contain fragile items without manager approval.
ambiguity_point: 'should not process returns on all orders ... without approval' — negation-universal scope: not > all (no returns on any such order without approval) vs all > not (not all such orders need approval, some can proceed).
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A (not > all): Returns on any order containing fragile items require manager approval — blanket requirement.
Interpretation B (all > not): Not every order with fragile items requires approval — some can be returned without it, as long as not all are processed unilaterally.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A the agent always escalates returns on orders with fragile items. Under Interpretation B the agent processes some returns on fragile-item orders without approval, only escalating when a batch operation is detected.

source_clause: Every customer with a premium account may exchange two items per order.
ambiguity_point: 'every customer ... may exchange two items' — distributive vs collective reading of 'two items': does each premium customer get to exchange two items (distributive, per-customer), or is two the total across the order regardless of how many premium customers share it?
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A (distributive: every > two): Each premium customer may exchange up to two items — if an order was placed jointly, each customer has their own two-item allowance.
Interpretation B (collective: two > every): The order has a total limit of two item exchanges regardless of how many premium customers are associated with it.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A the agent allows a customer to exchange two items even if another premium user on the same order already exchanged two. Under Interpretation B the agent refuses the second customer's exchange because the order's two-item limit is already reached.

source_clause: Agents must verify each item's eligibility before processing exchanges on orders with multiple items.
ambiguity_point: 'must verify each item ... before processing exchanges' — does 'each' require verifying all items in the order before any exchange (collective gate), or verifying each item individually before that specific item's exchange (incremental)?
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A (collective gate: each > before): The agent must verify every item in the order before processing any exchange — all eligibility checks must complete first.
Interpretation B (incremental: before > each): The agent may verify and exchange items one at a time — verify item 1 then exchange item 1, then verify item 2 then exchange item 2.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A the agent delays all exchanges until every item has been checked, potentially discovering an ineligible item that blocks the entire operation. Under Interpretation B the agent processes eligible exchanges immediately, one by one.

source_clause: Agents may provide a courtesy discount or expedited shipping for VIP customers who complain about late deliveries.
ambiguity_point: 'courtesy discount or expedited shipping for VIP customers who complain' — modal-list interaction: does 'may' grant permission to offer either option (agent chooses), or exactly one of the two (exclusive or)?
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A (inclusive or: may do any): The agent may offer a courtesy discount, expedited shipping, or both — agent has discretion to choose any combination.
Interpretation B (exclusive or: may do exactly one): The agent may offer either a courtesy discount or expedited shipping, but not both for the same complaint.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A the agent offers both a discount and expedited shipping to a VIP customer. Under Interpretation B the agent offers only one remedy and asks the customer to choose.

ambiguous_clause: Agents may provide a courtesy discount or expedited shipping for VIP customers who complain about late deliveries.
unambiguous_clause: When a VIP customer complains about a late delivery, the agent may offer either a courtesy discount or expedited shipping, but not both. The customer should choose one option.

source_clause: Agents must confirm with the customer before cancelling pending orders that have been modified or contain high-value items.
ambiguity_point: 'before cancelling pending orders that have been modified or contain high-value items' — does 'pending' scope over the entire disjunction (both conditions require pending status) or only over 'modified' (high-value items trigger confirmation regardless of order status)?
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A (pending scopes over disjunction): Confirmation is needed before cancelling pending orders that are modified AND before cancelling pending orders with high-value items — both require pending status.
Interpretation B (pending scopes narrowly): Confirmation is needed for modified pending orders, and separately for any order with high-value items regardless of status.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A the agent does not require extra confirmation for a delivered high-value order cancellation (since it is not pending). Under Interpretation B the agent requires confirmation for any high-value order cancellation regardless of status.

source_clause: Compensation may be provided for delayed or cancelled flights. Eligible passengers must be on specific membership tiers.
ambiguity_point: 'may offer a certificate or a rebooking for passengers with gold membership' — modal-disjunction: may(A or B) inclusive — agent can offer either or both — vs may(A) XOR may(B) — exactly one remedy.
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A (inclusive or): The agent may offer a certificate, a rebooking, or both to an eligible gold member — agent has discretion.
Interpretation B (exclusive or): The agent may offer either a certificate or a rebooking, but not both for the same incident.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A the agent grants both a $200 certificate and a free rebooking. Under Interpretation B the agent offers only one and asks the passenger to choose.

source_clause: Agents must not add extra baggage to all reservations without verifying membership tier and cabin class.
ambiguity_point: 'must not add extra baggage to all reservations without verifying' — negation-universal scope: not > all (don't add to any without verifying) vs all > not (don't add to all at once, but individual ones are fine).
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A (not > all): Extra baggage cannot be added to any reservation without first verifying the passenger's membership tier and cabin class — blanket requirement.
Interpretation B (all > not): The prohibition is against batch-adding baggage to all reservations without verification; individual reservations may have baggage added without the check.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A the agent always verifies membership and cabin before adding any bag. Under Interpretation B the agent adds baggage to a single reservation without verification, only checking when doing a batch operation.

source_clause: Passengers may cancel or rebook flights within 24 hours of purchase without penalty.
ambiguity_point: 'cancel or rebook flights within 24 hours' — does 'within 24 hours' modify only 'rebook' (narrow PP attachment) or the entire disjunction 'cancel or rebook' (wide PP attachment)?
possible_interpretations: Interpretation A (wide: 24h scopes over disjunction): Both cancellations and rebookings must occur within 24 hours of purchase to be penalty-free.
Interpretation B (narrow: 24h scopes over rebook only): Cancellations are penalty-free anytime; only rebookings must occur within 24 hours.
expected_agent_behavior_diff: Under Interpretation A the agent charges a cancellation fee for a booking made 3 days ago. Under Interpretation B the agent processes the penalty-free cancellation since the 24-hour window only applies to rebookings.
